
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** The arrow labels use plain uppercase text (e.g., `w sub j equals 1 i equals 1`) instead of complex subscripts, ensuring the Mermaid compiler renders the diagram without parsing errors. The meaning is unambiguous: weight from input neuron $i=1$ to hidden neuron $j=1$ is denoted $w_{11}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define a Back Propagation Network. List any four salient features of its architecture.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Back Propagation Network (BPN)** is a multi-layer, feedforward, fully-connected artificial neural network trained using the *backpropagation algorithm* — a supervised, gradient-descent-based, error-correction learning procedure.
>
> **Four salient architectural features:**
> 1. **Multi-layer topology** — Contains one or more *hidden layers* between input and output layers (unlike the single-layer perceptron).
> 2. **Strictly feedforward** — Information flows *only* from input → hidden → output; no recurrent or lateral connections exist in the classical BPN.
> 3. **Fully interlayer connected** — Every neuron in layer $(l)$ is connected to *every* neuron in layer $(l+1)$ with a learnable weight.
> 4. **Differentiability** — Uses *smooth, non-linear, differentiable* activation functions (sigmoid / tanh) so the chain rule can be applied during the backward pass.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Compare the single-layer perceptron with the back propagation network. State one problem that BPN can solve but the perceptron cannot.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Layer Perceptron | Back Propagation Network |
|---|---|---|
| Number of layers | 2 (input + output only) | 3 or more (input + hidden + output) |
| Hidden layers | None | One or more |
| Activation function | Step (sign) function | Sigmoid / tanh (differentiable) |
| Learning algorithm | Perceptron rule | Gradient descent + chain rule (backprop) |
| Problem solvable | Linearly separable (AND, OR) | Linearly **and** non-linearly separable (XOR) |
| Training | Converges in finite steps | Iterative, may require many epochs |

**Problem BPN can solve that perceptron cannot:** The **XOR (exclusive-OR)** problem, because XOR is not linearly separable. The perceptron's decision boundary is a single straight line, whereas BPN creates a *non-linear* decision region via the hidden layer's curved activation surfaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the back propagation algorithm for a multi-layer feedforward network. Clearly show the chain rule steps for both the output layer and the hidden layer weight updates.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define notations and forward pass equations.**

Let the BPN have input layer ($L_1$), one hidden layer ($L_2$), and output layer ($L_3$). For pattern $p$:

$$
v_j^{(p)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ji}^{(p)} x_i^{(p)} + b_j
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
z_j^{(p)} = f(v_j^{(p)})
$$

$$
v_k^{(p)} = \sum_{j=1}^{h} w'_{kj}^{(p)} z_j^{(p)} + b'_k
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
y_k^{(p)} = f(v_k^{(p)})
$$

**Step 2 — Error function.**

$$
E^{(p)} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} \left(d_k^{(p)} - y_k^{(p)}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Output layer weight update using chain rule.**

$$
\frac{\partial E^{(p)}}{\partial w'_{kj}} = \frac{\partial E^{(p)}}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial v_k} \cdot \frac{\partial v_k}{\partial w'_{kj}}
$$

$$
= -\left(d_k - y_k\right) \cdot f'(v_k) \cdot z_j
$$

Define the output layer local gradient:

$$
\delta_k^{(p)} = \left(d_k - y_k\right) f'(v_k)
$$

Hence the weight update:

$$
\boxed{\,w'_{kj}^{\,new} = w'_{kj}^{\,old} + \eta \cdot \delta_k^{(p)} \cdot z_j^{(p)}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Hidden layer weight update using multivariable chain rule.**

$$
\frac{\partial E^{(p)}}{\partial w_{ji}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial v_k} \cdot \frac{\partial v_k}{\partial z_j} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j}{\partial v_j} \cdot \frac{\partial v_j}{\partial w_{ji}}
$$

$$
= \sum_k \left[-\left(d_k - y_k\right) f'(v_k) \cdot w'_{kj} \cdot f'(v_j) \cdot x_i\right]
$$

Define the hidden layer local gradient:

$$
\delta_j^{(p)} = f'(v_j) \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(p)} \cdot w'_{kj}
$$

Hence the weight update:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{ji}^{\,new} = w_{ji}^{\,old} + \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(p)} \cdot x_i^{(p)}\,}
$$

The **recursion** $\delta_j$ depends on the *upstream* $\delta_k$ — this is precisely the "back propagation" of the error signal. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) Draw and explain the architecture of a Back Propagation Network. Compare the unipolar and bipolar sigmoid activation functions used in BPN.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

A BPN consists of three types of layers:

- **Input Layer:** Distributes the input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ to the next layer. *No computation* is performed here.
- **Hidden Layer(s):** Each neuron computes the weighted sum $v_j = \sum w_{ji} x_i + b_j$ and applies the activation $z_j = f(v_j)$. The hidden layer is what gives the BPN its *non-linear representational power*.
- **Output Layer:** Produces the final prediction $y_k = f(v_k)$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, m$. In a classifier, the index $k$ of the maximum $y_k$ is the predicted class.

The **bias** $b_j$ is connected to every neuron (except input) and is equivalent to a weight from a constant-1 input.

**Comparison of activation functions:**

| Property | Unipolar Sigmoid (Logistic) | Bipolar Sigmoid (Tanh) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $f(v) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-av}}$ | $f(v) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-av}}{1 + e^{-av}} = \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{av}{2}\right)$ |
| Output Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, +1)$ |
| Zero-centered | **No** — outputs are always positive | **Yes** — symmetric about origin |
| Derivative | $a \cdot y (1 - y)$ | $\dfrac{a}{2}(1 - y^2)$ |
| Use case | Output layer when targets are probabilities | Hidden layers (preferred in modern deep learning) |
| KTU preference | More common in KTU exam problems | Used in advanced derivations |

**Advantages of bipolar sigmoid over unipolar:** Zero-centered outputs lead to faster convergence because the *average activation* is zero, reducing zig-zagging of gradients during weight updates. $\blacksquare$

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the forward pass and backward pass in the back propagation algorithm with suitable mathematical equations. State the role of the learning rate $\eta$ and the momentum term $\alpha$.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Forward Pass:** Input pattern $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into the network. Each neuron in the hidden and output layers computes its *net input* and *activation* in sequence. Mathematically:

$$
v_j^{(l)} = \sum_{i} w_{ji}^{(l)} \cdot y_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)},
\qquad
y_j^{(l)} = f(v_j^{(l)})
$$

where $l$ is the layer index, $y_i^{(0)} = x_i$ for the input layer. The final $y_k^{(L)}$ is the network's prediction.

**Backward Pass:** The error between prediction $y_k$ and target $d_k$ is computed at the output layer. This error is *propagated backward* using the chain rule to compute the local gradient $\delta_j$ at *every* layer:

$$
\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - y_k) f'(v_k)
\quad\text{(output layer)}
$$

$$
\delta_j^{(l)} = f'(v_j^{(l)}) \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(l+1)} w_{kj}^{(l+1)}
\quad\text{(hidden layers, recursive)}
$$

The weight update is then applied:

$$
\Delta w_{ji}^{(l)} = \eta \cdot \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot y_i^{(l-1)}
$$

**Role of learning rate $\eta$ (0 < $\eta$ < 1):**
- Controls the *step size* of each weight update.
- Small $\eta$ → slow but stable convergence.
- Large $\eta$ → fast but oscillatory; may diverge.
- **Typical value:** $\eta = 0.1$ to $0.5$.

**Role of momentum term $\alpha$ (0 $\le$ $\alpha$ < 1):**
- Adds a fraction of the *previous* weight change to the current one:
$$
\Delta w(t+1) = \eta \cdot \delta \cdot y + \alpha \cdot \Delta w(t)
$$
- Smooths oscillations, accelerates convergence in long narrow valleys of the error surface.
- **Typical value:** $\alpha = 0.8$ to $0.9$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) A 2-2-1 BPN has the following initial weights, biases, input, and target. Use $\eta = 0.5$ and unipolar sigmoid with $a = 1$. Perform ONE complete forward pass and ONE complete backward pass, showing all updated weights.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
d = 1, \quad
\eta = 0.5, \quad
f(v) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-v}}, \quad f'(v) = y(1-y)
$$

Hidden weights (with biases in column 0):

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(h)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Output weights (with bias in row 0):

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(o)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.8 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Forward pass to hidden layer.**

$$
v_1^{(h)} = 0.1(1) + 0.3(0) + 0.2(1) = 0.30
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
z_1 = f(0.30) = 0.5744
$$

$$
v_2^{(h)} = 0.4(1) + 0.5(0) + 0.6(1) = 1.00
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
z_2 = f(1.00) = 0.7311
$$

**Step 2 — Forward pass to output layer.**

$$
v^{(o)} = 0.7(1) + 0.8(0.5744) + 0.9(0.7311) = 0.7 + 0.4595 + 0.6580 = 1.8175
$$

$$
y = f(1.8175) = 0.8602
$$

**Step 3 — Error computation.**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.8602)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.0196) = 0.0098
$$

**Step 4 — Output layer backward pass.**

$$
\delta^{(o)} = (1 - 0.8602) \cdot (0.8602)(1 - 0.8602) = (0.1398)(0.1201) = 0.01679
$$

**Step 5 — Update output weights.**

$$
\Delta w'_{10} = 0.5 \cdot 0.01679 \cdot 1 = 0.00840 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w'_{10}^{new} = 0.70840
$$

$$
\Delta w'_{11} = 0.5 \cdot 0.01679 \cdot 0.5744 = 0.00482 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w'_{11}^{new} = 0.80482
$$

$$
\Delta w'_{12} = 0.5 \cdot 0.01679 \cdot 0.7311 = 0.00614 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w'_{12}^{new} = 0.90614
$$

**Step 6 — Hidden layer backward pass.**

$$
\delta_1^{(h)} = (0.5744)(1 - 0.5744) \cdot 0.01679 \cdot 0.8 = (0.2444)(0.01343) = 0.00328
$$

$$
\delta_2^{(h)} = (0.7311)(1 - 0.7311) \cdot 0.01679 \cdot 0.9 = (0.1966)(0.01511) = 0.00297
$$

**Step 7 — Update hidden weights.**

$$
\Delta w_{10} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00328 \cdot 1 = 0.00164 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{10}^{new} = 0.10164
$$

$$
\Delta w_{11} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00328 \cdot 1 = 0.00164 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{11}^{new} = 0.30164
$$

$$
\Delta w_{12} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00328 \cdot 0 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{12}^{new} = 0.20000
$$

$$
\Delta w_{20} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00297 \cdot 1 = 0.00149 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{20}^{new} = 0.40149
$$

$$
\Delta w_{21} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00297 \cdot 1 = 0.00149 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{21}^{new} = 0.50149
$$

$$
\Delta w_{22} = 0.5 \cdot 0.00297 \cdot 0 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad w_{22}^{new} = 0.60000
$$

**Final updated weight matrices:**

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(h)}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.10164 & 0.30164 & 0.20000 \\ 0.40149 & 0.50149 & 0.60000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(o)}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.70840 \\ 0.80482 \\ 0.90614 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final error after one epoch:** $E_{new} = 0.0098$ (slight reduction from initial value). $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 3–5 Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in the error function.** This propagates an extra factor of 2 into ALL weight updates. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Sign error in the chain rule.** A common mistake is writing $+\delta_k$ instead of $-\delta_k$ in the gradient, leading to weight updates that *increase* the error. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Confusing $\delta$ (local gradient) with $\Delta w$ (weight change).** Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this. $\delta$ is a *neuron-level* quantity; $\Delta w$ is a *weight-level* quantity.
> 4. **Forgetting to update biases.** Biases must be updated using the same $\eta \cdot \delta$ rule with a constant input of 1. Many students skip this entirely. Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Sigmoid derivative computed at the wrong argument.** It should be $f'(v_j)$ — the *net input* — not $f'(y_j)$ — the *output*. Use $f'(v) = y(1-y)$ because the output $y$ already equals $f(v)$.
> 6. **Numerical arithmetic mistakes.** In multi-step numerical problems, even one rounding error in Step 3 propagates to ALL subsequent steps. KTU examiners are lenient if the *method* is correct, but they WILL deduct for sloppy arithmetic. Always show **3–4 decimal places** in intermediate steps.
> 7. **Not stating the convergence criterion.** End your answer with: *"Training continues until $E_{total} < \varepsilon$ or maximum epochs are reached."* Examiners love this closing line; it is often worth 0.5–1 bonus mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print This & Carry to Exam Hall):**

- ✅ BPN = Multi-layer **+** Feedforward **+** Fully-connected **+** Differentiable **+** Supervised (mnemonic: M-F-F-D-S).
- ✅ At least **one hidden layer** is mandatory (single-layer perceptron cannot solve XOR).
- ✅ **Forward pass:** $v_j = \sum w_{ji} x_i + b_j$, then $y_j = f(v_j)$ — apply layer by layer from input to output.
- ✅ **Error function:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \sum (d_k - y_k)^2$ — the $\frac{1}{2}$ is essential.
- ✅ **Output layer gradient:** $\delta_k = (d_k - y_k) \cdot f'(v_k)$.
- ✅ **Hidden layer gradient (recursive!):** $\delta_j = f'(v_j) \cdot \sum_k \delta_k w_{kj}$.
- ✅ **Weight update:** $w_{ji}^{new} = w_{ji}^{old} + \eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot y_i$.
- ✅ **Sigmoid derivative (unipolar):** $f'(v) = a \cdot y(1-y)$.
- ✅ **Sigmoid derivative (bipolar):** $f'(v) = \frac{a}{2}(1 - y^2)$.
- ✅ **Bias update:** same as weight update with constant input $x_0 = 1$, so $\Delta b_j = \eta \cdot \delta_j$.
- ✅ **Momentum term:** $\Delta w(t+1) = \eta \delta y + \alpha \Delta w(t)$, with $\alpha \in [0, 1)$.
- ✅ **Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko 1989):** A single hidden layer with sufficient neurons can approximate *any* continuous function.
- ✅ **Stopping criteria:** error threshold $\varepsilon$, max epochs, gradient norm, or validation error plateau.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient:** hidden-layer $\delta$ values are typically $\sim 10\times$ smaller than output $\delta$ (this is the famous vanishing gradient problem in deep networks).
- ✅ **Numerical example:** practice the 2-2-1 numerical problem until you can do it in under 20 minutes.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Module 2 weightage:** Approximately 15–20% of the entire paper (Module 2 is the most heavily weighted).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** If the BPN question offers internal choice, prefer the **derivation question (Question A-a)** because it has a *fixed, deterministic* answer and you can score full marks by writing the chain rule correctly. Avoid the numerical computation question unless you have practiced it multiple times — a single arithmetic slip can cost 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Training Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Training Algorithm — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training Algorithm (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Training Algorithm* in Artificial Neural Networks is a systematic, iterative mathematical procedure that adjusts the **synaptic weights** $w$ and **bias terms** $b$ of a neural network so as to minimize a predefined **loss function** (or cost function) $E(\mathbf{w})$, thereby enabling the network to learn the underlying input–output mapping from a given set of training examples.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, training algorithms form the computational backbone of supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning paradigms. They govern **how** a neural network transforms raw data into learned intelligence by optimizing internal parameters.

The general optimization objective is expressed as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}} E(\mathbf{w}) = \min_{\mathbf{w}} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} L(y_n, \hat{y}_n)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w}$ = vector of all trainable parameters (weights + biases)
- $E(\mathbf{w})$ = empirical risk / cost function
- $L(\cdot)$ = per-sample loss
- $y_n$ = true label, $\hat{y}_n$ = predicted output
- $N$ = number of training samples

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Learning to Ride a Bicycle**
>
> Imagine a child learning to ride a bicycle:
> - **Initial attempt:** The child wobbles and falls (high error).
> - **Each fall:** The brain receives a feedback signal (pain + imbalance).
> - **Weight adjustment:** Tiny corrections are made — lean slightly left, turn handlebar right, pedal harder.
> - **Convergence:** After many attempts, the child can ride smoothly (minimum error).
>
> The "weights" in the brain are analogous to the **synaptic strengths** in a neural network. The training algorithm is the *learning rule* that quantifies exactly **how much** and **in which direction** to adjust these weights after every mistake.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Training Algorithms (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Category | Key Algorithms | Learning Type |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Error-Correction Based** | Perceptron Rule, Delta Rule (LMS / Widrow–Hoff) | Supervised |
| **Gradient-Based** | Backpropagation, Stochastic Gradient Descent, Batch GD, Mini-Batch GD | Supervised |
| **Hebbian-Based** | Hebb's Rule, Oja's Rule, Sanger's Rule | Unsupervised |
| **Competitive** | Kohonen Self-Organizing Maps (SOM), Learning Vector Quantization (LVQ) | Unsupervised |
| **Reinforcement** | Policy Gradient, Reward-weighted Regression | Reinforcement |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The primary training algorithms covered in this module are the **Perceptron Learning Rule**, **Delta Rule (LMS Algorithm)**, and the **Backpropagation Algorithm** for Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs).

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Training

- **Learning Rate ($\eta$):** Step size of weight update. Typical range: $0.01$ to $0.5$.
- **Epoch:** One complete pass through the entire training dataset.
- **Convergence:** Achieved when $E(\mathbf{w}) \le \epsilon$ (tolerance threshold).
- **Generalization Error:** Performance on unseen test data (true measure of learning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Training Algorithm — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Training Algorithm

Every training algorithm in KTU 2024 Module 2 follows a unified operational pipeline:

1. **Initialization:** Assign small random values to weights $\mathbf{w}^{(0)}$, set bias $b^{(0)}$, choose learning rate $\eta$.
2. **Forward Propagation:** Compute the network's predicted output $\hat{y}$ for a given input $\mathbf{x}$.
3. **Error Computation:** Calculate the loss $L(y, \hat{y})$ between predicted and true output.
4. **Backward Propagation of Error:** Compute the gradient $\nabla E(\mathbf{w})$ with respect to each weight using the **chain rule of calculus**.
5. **Weight Update Rule:** Apply the update equation $\mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$.
6. **Iteration:** Repeat steps 2–5 until convergence criteria are met.
7. **Validation:** Evaluate the trained model on a held-out validation set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** The negative gradient $-\nabla E(\mathbf{w})$ always points in the direction of *steepest descent* on the error surface. By moving weights opposite to the gradient, we iteratively descend toward a (local) minimum of the error landscape.

## 2.2 Three Canonical Training Algorithms — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: KTU 2024 Module 2 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet for Training Algorithms**

| Algorithm | Applicable Network | Weight Update Rule | Activation Function | Error Signal $\delta$ |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Perceptron Rule** | Single-layer, linearly separable | $\Delta w_i = \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$ | Step (signum) | $e = y - \hat{y}$ |
| **Delta Rule (LMS / Widrow–Hoff)** | Single-layer, ADALINE | $\Delta w_i = \eta (y - \phi(v)) x_i$ | Linear (identity) | $e = y - \phi(v)$ |
| **Backpropagation (Generalized Delta Rule)** | Multi-layer (MLP) | $\Delta w_{ij}^{(l)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}}$ | Sigmoid / Tanh / ReLU | $\delta_j^{(l)} = (e_j) \phi'(v_j)$ (output); $\delta_j^{(l)} = \left(\sum_k \delta_k^{(l+1)} w_{jk}^{(l+1)}\right) \phi'(v_j)$ (hidden) |

where:
- $v = \sum_i w_i x_i + b$ = induced local field (pre-activation)
- $\phi(\cdot)$ = activation function
- $\eta$ = learning rate (typically $0 < \eta \le 1$)
- $w_{ij}^{(l)}$ = weight from neuron $i$ in layer $(l-1)$ to neuron $j$ in layer $l$
- $\delta_j^{(l)}$ = local gradient at neuron $j$ in layer $l$

## 2.3 Mathematical Justification: Why Gradient Descent?

Consider a scalar error function $E(\mathbf{w})$. The Taylor series expansion around the current point $\mathbf{w}^{(t)}$ is:

$$E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)} + \Delta \mathbf{w}) \approx E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)}) + \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})^T \Delta \mathbf{w} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{H} \Delta \mathbf{w}$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is the Hessian matrix. The change in error is:

$$\Delta E \approx \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})^T \Delta \mathbf{w}$$

To **minimize** $\Delta E$ (i.e., make it maximally negative), we choose:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = -\eta \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$$

This guarantees $\Delta E = -\eta \Vert \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)}) \Vert^2 \le 0$, meaning error monotonically decreases.

## 2.4 Variants of Gradient Descent (KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three primary optimization variants tested frequently in KTU exams:**

### (i) Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
Uses the **entire dataset** to compute the gradient per update:

$$\mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla E_{\text{full}}(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$$

- **Pros:** Stable convergence, deterministic path.
- **Cons:** Slow for large datasets; memory-intensive.

### (ii) Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
Uses **one sample at a time**:

$$\mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla L_n(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$$

- **Pros:** Fast, can escape local minima due to noise.
- **Cons:** Noisy convergence path; may oscillate near minima.

### (iii) Mini-Batch Gradient Descent
Compromise using a mini-batch of size $m$:

$$\mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} - \eta \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla L_i(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$$

- **Pros:** Best of both worlds — stability + speed. Industry standard.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Training Algorithm Used | Why? |
|-------------------|------------------------|------|
| **Image Classification (CNNs)** | Backprop + Mini-Batch GD + Adam | Non-convex loss, large datasets |
| **Speech Recognition (RNNs)** | Backprop Through Time (BPTT) | Sequential temporal dependencies |
| **Embedded Control Systems** | Perceptron / Delta Rule | Linear separability, low compute |
| **Anomaly Detection** | SOM / Competitive Learning | Unsupervised, no labels |
| **Recommendation Engines** | Wide-Deep Networks + SGD | Sparse, high-dimensional data |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Training Algorithm — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Perceptron Learning Rule

### Setup
Consider a single-layer perceptron with $n$ inputs, weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$, bias $b$, and step activation:

$$\hat{y} = \phi(v) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } v \ge 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } v < 0 \end{cases}$$

where the induced local field is:

$$v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

### Derivation of the Update Rule

**Step 1:** Define the instantaneous error for sample $p$ as:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}(y_p - \hat{y}_p)^2$$

**Step 2:** The gradient of $E_p$ with respect to weight $w_i$ is:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial w_i} \left[ \frac{1}{2}(y_p - \hat{y}_p)^2 \right]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = (y_p - \hat{y}_p) \cdot \left( -\frac{\partial \hat{y}_p}{\partial w_i} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Since $\hat{y}_p = \phi(v_p)$ and $v_p = \sum_i w_i x_{i,p} + b$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_p}{\partial w_i} = \phi'(v_p) \cdot x_{i,p}$$

**Step 5:** For the step (signum) function, $\phi'(v_p) = 0$ almost everywhere — this makes direct gradient descent infeasible. Perceptron Rule **bypasses** the derivative by defining the error signal directly:

$$e_p = y_p - \hat{y}_p$$

**Step 6:** The Perceptron update rule is:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, e_p \, x_{i,p} = \eta (y_p - \hat{y}_p) x_{i,p}$$

**Step 7:** In incremental form (after processing sample $p$):

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} + \eta (y_p - \hat{y}_p) x_{i,p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Theorem (Rosenblatt, 1958):** If the training data is **linearly separable**, the perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a separating hyperplane in a finite number of steps.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Delta Rule (LMS / Widrow–Hoff)

The Delta Rule was developed by **Widrow and Hoff (1960)** to overcome the perceptron's limitation of using a non-differentiable activation function. It uses a **linear activation** $\phi(v) = v$ so that the gradient becomes well-defined.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Error for sample $p$:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}(y_p - \phi(v_p))^2$$

**Step 2:** Gradient with respect to $w_i$:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = (y_p - \phi(v_p)) \cdot \left( -\frac{\partial \phi(v_p)}{\partial w_i} \right)$$

**Step 3:** For linear activation, $\phi(v_p) = v_p = \sum_i w_i x_{i,p} + b$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi(v_p)}{\partial w_i} = x_{i,p}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = -(y_p - \phi(v_p)) \, x_{i,p} = -e_p \, x_{i,p}$$

**Step 5:** The weight update (gradient descent step) is:

$$\Delta w_i = -\eta \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = \eta (y_p - \phi(v_p)) x_{i,p}$$

**Step 6:** Final Delta Rule update:

$$\boxed{w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} + \eta (y_p - \phi(v_p)) x_{i,p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Delta Rule minimizes the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** continuously, even for non-linearly-separable data. However, since it uses a linear activation, it can only learn **linear** input–output mappings.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation Algorithm (Generalized Delta Rule)

For **multi-layer** networks, we need a method to compute gradients for hidden-layer weights — this is the **Backpropagation Algorithm**, formalized by **Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)**.

### Architecture
Consider a 3-layer MLP: Input (layer 0) → Hidden (layer 1) → Output (layer 2).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Network output at neuron $j$ in output layer:

$$\hat{y}_j = \phi_2(v_j^{(2)}) = \phi_2 \left( \sum_i w_{ij}^{(2)} \phi_1(v_i^{(1)}) \right)$$

**Step 2:** Total error across $C$ output neurons and $P$ training samples:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{p=1}^{P} \sum_{j=1}^{C} (y_{j,p} - \hat{y}_{j,p})^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the local gradient at output neuron $j$:

$$\delta_j^{(2)} = \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial v_j^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial \hat{y}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial v_j^{(2)}} = -(y_j - \hat{y}_j) \cdot \phi_2'(v_j^{(2)})$$

**Step 4:** Compute the local gradient at hidden neuron $i$ (chain rule applied backward):

$$\delta_i^{(1)} = \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial v_i^{(1)}} = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{C} \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial v_j^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial v_j^{(2)}}{\partial \phi_1(v_i^{(1)})} \cdot \frac{\partial \phi_1(v_i^{(1)})}{\partial v_i^{(1)}} \right)$$

$$\delta_i^{(1)} = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{C} \delta_j^{(2)} \, w_{ij}^{(2)} \right) \cdot \phi_1'(v_i^{(1)})$$

**Step 5:** Weight update for output layer weights:

$$\Delta w_{ij}^{(2)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_{ij}^{(2)}} = \eta \, \delta_j^{(2)} \, \phi_1(v_i^{(1)})$$

**Step 6:** Weight update for hidden layer weights:

$$\Delta w_{ki}^{(1)} = -\eta \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_{ki}^{(1)}} = \eta \, \delta_i^{(1)} \, x_k$$

**Step 7:** Update all weights in vectorized form:

$$w_{ij}^{(\ell, t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(\ell, t)} + \Delta w_{ij}^{(\ell, t)}$$

### Compact Algorithmic Pseudocode (Conceptual — Non-Programmatic)

```
REPEAT until convergence:
  1. FOR each training sample (x, y):
       a. FORWARD PASS:
          - Compute v_j^(l) and y_j^(l) for every neuron in every layer
       b. COMPUTE OUTPUT ERROR:
          - δ_j^(L) = (y_j - ŷ_j) · φ'(v_j^(L))
       c. BACKWARD PASS:
          - For each layer l = L-1, L-2, ..., 1:
              δ_j^(l) = (Σ_k δ_k^(l+1) · w_jk^(l+1)) · φ'(v_j^(l))
       d. UPDATE WEIGHTS:
          - w_ij^(l) ← w_ij^(l) + η · δ_j^(l) · y_i^(l-1)
          - b_j^(l)  ← b_j^(l)  + η · δ_j^(l)
  2. END FOR
END REPEAT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Passes of Backpropagation:**
> 1. **Forward Pass** — Inputs propagate layer-by-layer to produce output $\hat{y}$.
> 2. **Backward Pass** — Error signals $\delta^{(\ell)}$ propagate from output back to input, computing gradients along the way.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Training Algorithm — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Training Algorithm

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete end-to-end flow of a generic supervised training algorithm as specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize weights w and bias b randomly] --> B[Set learning rate eta and tolerance epsilon]
    B --> C[Load training dataset D = x_i, y_i]
    C --> D[Forward Propagation: Compute predicted output y_hat]
    D --> E[Loss Computation: Calculate E = Loss y, y_hat]
    E --> F{Convergence check: E less than epsilon?}
    F -- No --> G[Backward Propagation: Compute gradient of E w.r.t. w]
    G --> H[Compute Local Gradient delta for each layer]
    H --> I[Update Weights: w_new = w_old minus eta times gradient]
    I --> J[Update Bias: b_new = b_old minus eta times gradient]
    J --> K[Increment epoch counter]
    K --> D
    F -- Yes --> L[Validation: Evaluate on held-out test set]
    L --> M[Output: Trained Neural Network Model]
    M --> N[End]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#D81B60,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Comparative Algorithm Decision Flowchart

This diagram helps a KTU 2024 student decide which training algorithm to choose based on problem characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Training Algorithm] --> Q1{Is data linearly separable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is single layer sufficient?}
    Q1 -- No --> BP[Use Backpropagation with MLP]
    Q2 -- Yes --> PERC[Perceptron Learning Rule]
    Q2 -- No --> BP2[Use Backpropagation with MLP]
    Q1 -- Partial --> DELTA[Delta Rule LMS for ADALINE]

    PERC --> OUT1[Fast, simple, binary outputs]
    DELTA --> OUT2[Continuous outputs, linear regression]
    BP --> OUT3[Non-linear mappings, deep learning]

    style START fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style Q1 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FFA000,stroke-width:2px
    style PERC fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style DELTA fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px
    style BP fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Backpropagation Signal Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Vector x] --> H1[Hidden Layer 1: phi1, weights W1]
    H1 --> H2[Hidden Layer 2: phi2, weights W2]
    H2 --> OUT[Output Layer: phi_out, weights W_out]
    OUT --> LOSS[Loss Function E]
    LOSS -- delta_out --> H2
    H2 -- delta_2 --> H1
    H1 -- delta_1 --> W1
    LOSS -.Error Signal Backflow.-> H2
    H1 -.Error Signal Backflow.-> W1

    style X fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    style LOSS fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px
    style H2 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** show the forward propagation of activations. The **dashed arrows** show the backward propagation of error gradients ($\delta$). The chain rule of calculus applied backward through this graph is precisely what gives backpropagation its name.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Training Algorithm — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
> *What is meant by a training algorithm in artificial neural networks? List any two training algorithms used for supervised learning.*

**Model Answer:**

A training algorithm in artificial neural networks is a well-defined iterative mathematical procedure used to adjust the **synaptic weights** and **biases** of a neural network such that a predefined **error (loss) function** is minimized over a set of training examples. It enables the network to learn the input–output mapping by progressively refining its internal parameters.

The general form of the weight update rule is:

$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} + \Delta w^{(t)}$$

Two supervised training algorithms are:
1. **Perceptron Learning Rule** — used for linearly separable binary classification.
2. **Backpropagation Algorithm** — used for training multi-layer perceptrons with non-linear activation functions.

---

### Question 2
> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
> *Explain the significance of the learning rate $\eta$ in a training algorithm. What happens if $\eta$ is too large or too small?*

**Model Answer:**

The learning rate $\eta$ is a **hyperparameter** that controls the **step size** of the weight update during training. It determines how quickly or slowly a neural network learns.

$$\Delta w = \eta \cdot (\text{error signal})$$

**Significance:**
- It scales the magnitude of the weight correction at each iteration.
- It directly affects the **speed** and **stability** of convergence.

**Case Analysis:**

| Learning Rate | Effect on Training |
|---------------|-------------------|
| $\eta$ too small | Slow convergence; algorithm may get stuck in local minima; long training time |
| $\eta$ too large | Oscillations; divergence; the algorithm may overshoot the global minimum |
| $\eta$ optimal | Fast, stable convergence to a (local) minimum of the error surface |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Module 2]`**
> **(a)** Derive the **Perceptron Learning Rule** mathematically. State and explain the **Perceptron Convergence Theorem**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Delta Rule (Widrow–Hoff LMS Algorithm)** with full mathematical derivation. Compare it with the Perceptron Learning Rule. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Derivation of Perceptron Learning Rule:**

Consider a perceptron with input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, weight vector $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$, and bias $b$.

**Step 1:** Compute the induced local field:

$$v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Step 2:** Apply the bipolar step activation:

$$\hat{y} = \phi(v) = \begin{cases} +1, & v \ge 0 \\ -1, & v < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Define the error signal for sample $p$:

$$e_p = y_p - \hat{y}_p$$

**Step 4:** The perceptron update rule is:

$$\boxed{w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} + \eta (y_p - \hat{y}_p) \, x_{i,p}}$$

**Step 5:** Bias update:

$$b^{(t+1)} = b^{(t)} + \eta (y_p - \hat{y}_p)$$

**Perceptron Convergence Theorem (Rosenblatt, 1958):**
*If the training samples are linearly separable, the perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a separating weight vector in a finite number of iterations.*

**Limitations:**
- Fails for non-linearly separable data (e.g., XOR problem).
- Convergence may be slow.
- Solution is not unique — depends on initialization.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Delta Rule (LMS Algorithm) Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Use a **linear activation** $\phi(v) = v$ to ensure differentiability.

**Step 2:** Define the instantaneous squared error:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}(y_p - \phi(v_p))^2 = \frac{1}{2}(y_p - v_p)^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient with respect to $w_i$:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = -(y_p - v_p) \cdot \frac{\partial v_p}{\partial w_i} = -(y_p - v_p) \cdot x_{i,p}$$

**Step 4:** Apply gradient descent:

$$\Delta w_i = -\eta \frac{\partial E_p}{\partial w_i} = \eta (y_p - v_p) x_{i,p}$$

**Step 5:** Final update:

$$\boxed{w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} + \eta (y_p - v_p) x_{i,p}}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Perceptron Rule | Delta Rule (LMS) |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Activation | Bipolar step (non-differentiable) | Linear (differentiable) |
| Error minimization | Discrete (misclassification count) | Continuous (MSE) |
| Gradient computation | Not possible (bypassed) | Full gradient descent |
| Convergence | Finite (linearly separable data) | Asymptotic (always converges) |
| Handles non-separable data | No (oscillates) | Yes (minimizes MSE) |
| Network | Perceptron | ADALINE |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]`**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Backpropagation Algorithm**. Derive the update rule for weights in the **output layer** and the **hidden layer** of a multi-layer perceptron. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the three variants of **Gradient Descent** — Batch, Stochastic, and Mini-Batch. Discuss their advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Backpropagation Algorithm:**

Consider a 3-layer MLP with input layer, one hidden layer, and output layer.

**Step 1: Forward Pass**

For hidden layer neuron $j$:

$$v_j^{(1)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}$$

$$z_j^{(1)} = \phi_1(v_j^{(1)})$$

For output layer neuron $k$:

$$v_k^{(2)} = \sum_j w_{jk}^{(2)} z_j^{(1)} + b_k^{(2)}$$

$$\hat{y}_k = \phi_2(v_k^{(2)})$$

**Step 2: Compute Output Error**

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{C} (y_k - \hat{y}_k)^2$$

**Step 3: Output Layer Local Gradient**

$$\delta_k^{(2)} = (y_k - \hat{y}_k) \cdot \phi_2'(v_k^{(2)})$$

**Step 4: Hidden Layer Local Gradient (backpropagated)**

$$\delta_j^{(1)} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{C} \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)} \right) \cdot \phi_1'(v_j^{(1)})$$

**Step 5: Output Layer Weight Update**

$$\boxed{w_{jk}^{(2, t+1)} = w_{jk}^{(2, t)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(2)} \, z_j^{(1)}}$$

**Step 6: Hidden Layer Weight Update**

$$\boxed{w_{ij}^{(1, t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(1, t)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)} \, x_i}$$

**Step 7: Bias Updates**

$$b_k^{(2, t+1)} = b_k^{(2, t)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(2)}$$

$$b_j^{(1, t+1)} = b_j^{(1, t)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Three Variants of Gradient Descent:**

| Variant | Update Rule | Batch Size | Pros | Cons |
|---------|-------------|-----------|------|------|
| **Batch GD** | $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla E_{\text{full}}$ | $N$ (full) | Stable, deterministic | Slow, memory-intensive |
| **Stochastic GD** | $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla L_i$ | $1$ | Fast, escapes local minima | Noisy oscillations |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla L_i$ | $m$ (e.g., 32, 64, 256) | Balanced speed & stability | Requires tuning of $m$ |

**Detailed Discussion:**

- **Batch GD:** Computes the gradient over the entire training set before each update. Provides a smooth, monotonic error reduction curve. Used in small datasets where exact gradient computation is feasible.

- **Stochastic GD:** Updates weights after each training example. Introduces stochasticity that helps escape shallow local minima. Learning rate schedules (e.g., $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \text{decay} \cdot t)$) are often used.

- **Mini-Batch GD:** The de-facto standard in deep learning. Enables vectorized GPU computation, balances noise and stability. Common batch sizes: 32, 64, 128, 256.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the derivative of activation:** Students often write $\delta_j$ without the $\phi'(v_j)$ term. This is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in backpropagation derivations.
> 2. **Confusing error signal vs. local gradient:** The error $e = y - \hat{y}$ is NOT the same as the local gradient $\delta = e \cdot \phi'(v)$.
> 3. **Wrong index convention:** Always be consistent with your indices — distinguish input layer ($i$), hidden layer ($j$), output layer ($k$).
> 4. **Skipping bias updates:** Many students update weights but forget to update biases. Both must be updated.
> 5. **Misquoting convergence criteria:** Convergence is achieved when $\Vert \nabla E \Vert < \epsilon$ OR when $E < \epsilon$, NOT after a fixed number of epochs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Rapid Revision Checklist for "Training Algorithm"**

### Core Concepts
- A **training algorithm** adjusts weights to minimize the loss function $E(\mathbf{w})$.
- The general form: $w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \nabla E(\mathbf{w}^{(t)})$.

### Three Pillars of Module 2
- **Perceptron Rule:** $\Delta w = \eta (y - \hat{y}) x$; works only for linearly separable data; uses non-differentiable step activation.
- **Delta Rule (LMS):** $\Delta w = \eta (y - v) x$; uses linear activation; minimizes MSE continuously.
- **Backpropagation:** Generalizes Delta Rule to multi-layer networks; uses chain rule to compute $\delta$ for hidden layers.

### Critical Mathematical Identities
- Output layer local gradient: $\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - \hat{y}_k) \cdot \phi'(v_k^{(L)})$.
- Hidden layer local gradient: $\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \left( \sum_k \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} w_{jk}^{(\ell+1)} \right) \cdot \phi'(v_j^{(\ell)})$.
- Weight update: $w_{ij}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(\ell)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, y_i^{(\ell-1)}$.

### Hyperparameter Awareness
- **Learning rate $\eta$:** controls step size. Too small = slow; too large = divergence.
- **Epoch:** one full pass over the training set.
- **Tolerance $\epsilon$:** convergence threshold for $E$ or $\Vert \nabla E \Vert$.

### Key Theorems
- **Perceptron Convergence Theorem:** Guarantees finite-step convergence for linearly separable data.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A 2-layer MLP with sufficient hidden neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain.

### Algorithm Selection Heuristic
- Linearly separable + binary → **Perceptron**.
- Linear regression → **Delta Rule / LMS**.
- Non-linear mapping + multi-layer → **Backpropagation**.
- Large-scale deep learning → **Backprop + Mini-Batch GD + Adam/RMSProp**.

### Frequently Tested KTU Topics
- Derivation of the three update rules.
- Comparison of BGD, SGD, Mini-Batch GD.
- Identifying the role of $\eta$, $\phi$, $\delta$.
- Tracing a single forward + backward pass through a small numerical example.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Training Algorithm.**
> *For numerical practice, set up a 2-2-1 MLP with sigmoid activations, initialize weights to small random values, choose $\eta = 0.5$, and trace one forward + backward pass on a single training example. This is the most common KTU 2024 numerical question pattern.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 2 Problem Landscape — What Are We Solving?

In **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)**, Module 2 typically focuses on the *mathematical heart* of how networks actually *learn* — that is, **Perceptron Models, Activation Functions, Gradient Descent, and the Backpropagation Algorithm**. The "Problems" topic consolidates numerical and analytical exercises that test a student's ability to:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Problems* in Module 2 refer to **numerical and analytical exercises** that demonstrate the working of single-layer perceptron training, multi-layer perceptron (MLP) forward pass, error backpropagation, weight/bias updation using gradient-based optimization, and convergence analysis using learning rate dynamics.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Student Climbing Down a Mountain

Imagine a student blindfolded on a misty mountain, trying to reach the valley (minimum error). Each step they take is determined by:

- **The slope under their feet** → the **gradient** of the error function
- **The size of their step** → the **learning rate** $\eta$
- **The direction they face** → the **negative gradient** direction (steepest descent)

This is *exactly* what gradient descent does in training neural networks. The "Problems" we solve are mathematical journeys down this mountain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Module 2 Problems require mastery of:
> 1. **Perceptron Convergence Algorithm** (Rosenblatt's rule)
> 2. **Delta Rule / LMS Algorithm** (Widrow-Hoff)
> 3. **Backpropagation (Generalized Delta Rule)**
> 4. **Activation Function Derivatives** (Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU)
> 5. **Numerical Weight Updates** with given datasets

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | **0.01 – 0.5** |
| Number of Epochs | $E$ | **100 – 10,000** |
| Convergence Error | $E_{min}$ | **0.001 – 0.01** |
| Momentum Coefficient | $\alpha$ | **0.5 – 0.9** |
| Weight Initialization Range | $w$ | **[-1, 1]** or **[-0.5, 0.5]** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Logical Flow of Problem-Solving

The standard KTU Module 2 problem can be broken into **5 canonical steps**:

1. **Initialize** weights $w$ and bias $b$ (often randomly or to zero).
2. **Forward Propagate** the input through the network to compute the output $\hat{y}$.
3. **Compute the Error** $E$ between predicted $\hat{y}$ and target $y$.
4. **Backpropagate** the error — calculate $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ and $\frac{\partial E}{\partial b}$ using the **chain rule**.
5. **Update Weights** using the gradient descent rule.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- *Why forward propagate?* — To get a prediction we can compare with the truth.
- *Why compute error?* — Error is the *loss landscape*; without it, no learning is possible.
- *Why backpropagate?* — To assign "blame" to each weight for its contribution to the error.
- *Why update weights?* — To *minimize* the error iteratively.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This for KTU Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Perceptron Net Input | $net = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Linear combiner |
| 2 | Step Activation | $f(net) = \begin{cases} 1 & net \geq \theta \\ 0 & net < \theta \end{cases}$ | Binary output |
| 3 | Perceptron Weight Update | $\Delta w_i = \eta (y - \hat{y}) x_i$ | Rosenblatt rule |
| 4 | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | Range: $(0,1)$ |
| 5 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)$ | Key for backprop |
| 6 | Tanh Activation | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | Range: $(-1,1)$ |
| 7 | Tanh Derivative | $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | — |
| 8 | ReLU Activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Range: $[0, \infty)$ |
| 9 | ReLU Derivative | $f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | — |
| 10 | Mean Squared Error | $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k}(y_k - \hat{y}_k)^2$ | Common loss |
| 11 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $E = -\sum_{k} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ | Classification |
| 12 | Delta Rule (LMS) | $\Delta w_i = \eta (y - \hat{y}) f'(net) x_i$ | Gradient descent |
| 13 | Backprop Hidden Layer | $\delta_h = f'_h(net_h) \sum_k w_{hk} \delta_k$ | Error propagation |
| 14 | Weight Update (General) | $w_{ij}^{new} = w_{ij}^{old} + \Delta w_{ij}$ | Iterative |
| 15 | Gradient Descent | $w := w - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ | Minimization |
| 16 | Momentum Update | $v := \alpha v + \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}, \quad w := w - v$ | Faster convergence |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These problems are not merely academic — they underpin:
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (cancer detection via MLP)
- **Autonomous Vehicle Perception** (convolutional + backprop-trained networks)
- **Financial Forecasting** (LSTM/GRU weight updates)
- **Speech Recognition** (Deep Neural Network acoustic modeling)
- **Industrial Process Control** (adaptive perceptron-based controllers)

> [!NOTE]
> *Engineering Insight:* Every time a smartphone camera detects a face, it is using a backpropagation-trained CNN whose weights were tuned using exactly the formulas above.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PROBLEM TYPE 1: Perceptron Weight Update (Rosenblatt's Algorithm)

### Problem Statement
Train a single-layer perceptron using the **Perceptron Learning Rule** to classify the following data. Use learning rate $\eta = 1$, initial weights $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = -0.1$, bias $b = 0.2$, and threshold $\theta = 0.2$.

| Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Target $t$ |
|--------|-------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**STEP 1: Initialize Parameters**

$$w_1^{(0)} = 0.3, \quad w_2^{(0)} = -0.1, \quad b^{(0)} = 0.2, \quad \eta = 1, \quad \theta = 0.2$$

**STEP 2: Compute Net Input for Sample 1** $(x_1=1, x_2=1, t=1)$

$$net^{(1)} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.3)(1) + (-0.1)(1) + 0.2 = 0.4$$

Since $net^{(1)} = 0.4 \geq \theta = 0.2$:

$$\hat{y}^{(1)} = 1$$

Compare with target $t = 1$ → **No weight change needed** (correctly classified).

**STEP 3: Process Sample 2** $(x_1=0, x_2=1, t=0)$

$$net^{(2)} = (0.3)(0) + (-0.1)(1) + 0.2 = 0.1$$

Since $0.1 < 0.2$:

$$\hat{y}^{(2)} = 0$$

Compare with target $t = 0$ → **No weight change needed** (correctly classified).

**STEP 4: Process Sample 3** $(x_1=1, x_2=0, t=0)$

$$net^{(3)} = (0.3)(1) + (-0.1)(0) + 0.2 = 0.5$$

Since $0.5 \geq 0.2$:

$$\hat{y}^{(3)} = 1$$

But target $t = 0$ → **Misclassification!** Apply weight update:

$$\Delta w_1 = \eta(t - \hat{y})x_1 = 1 \cdot (0 - 1) \cdot 1 = -1$$
$$\Delta w_2 = \eta(t - \hat{y})x_2 = 1 \cdot (0 - 1) \cdot 0 = 0$$
$$\Delta b = \eta(t - \hat{y}) = -1$$

Updated weights:

$$w_1^{(1)} = 0.3 + (-1) = -0.7$$
$$w_2^{(1)} = -0.1 + 0 = -0.1$$
$$b^{(1)} = 0.2 + (-1) = -0.8$$

**STEP 5: Process Sample 4** $(x_1=0, x_2=0, t=0)$

$$net^{(4)} = (-0.7)(0) + (-0.1)(0) + (-0.8) = -0.8$$

Since $-0.8 < 0.2$:

$$\hat{y}^{(4)} = 0$$

Target is $0$ → **Correct.** No update.

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm continues through multiple epochs until all samples are correctly classified, or a maximum iteration count is reached.

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM TYPE 2: Backpropagation Numerical Problem (Multi-Layer PerNet)

### Problem Statement
Consider a **2-2-1 feedforward network** (2 inputs, 2 hidden neurons, 1 output). Given:

- Input vector: $\mathbf{x} = [0.5, \; 0.3]^T$
- Target output: $t = 0.8$
- Initial weights (input → hidden): $w_{11} = 0.2, w_{12} = 0.4, w_{21} = 0.1, w_{22} = 0.3$
- Initial weights (hidden → output): $w_{31} = 0.5, w_{32} = 0.6$
- Bias hidden: $b_1 = 0.1, b_2 = 0.2$; Bias output: $b_3 = 0.15$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$
- Activation function: **Sigmoid** for all neurons

Perform **one iteration of forward pass + backpropagation** and update all weights.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**STEP 1: Forward Pass — Hidden Layer Net Inputs**

$$net_{h1} = w_{11}x_1 + w_{21}x_2 + b_1 = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) + 0.1 = 0.23$$
$$net_{h2} = w_{12}x_1 + w_{22}x_2 + b_2 = (0.4)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.3) + 0.2 = 0.49$$

**STEP 2: Apply Sigmoid Activation to Hidden Layer**

$$h_1 = \sigma(net_{h1}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.23}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7945} = 0.5572$$
$$h_2 = \sigma(net_{h2}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.49}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6126} = 0.6201$$

**STEP 3: Forward Pass — Output Layer Net Input**

$$net_{out} = w_{31}h_1 + w_{32}h_2 + b_3 = (0.5)(0.5572) + (0.6)(0.6201) + 0.15 = 0.8006$$

**STEP 4: Apply Sigmoid Activation to Output**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(net_{out}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.8006}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4491} = 0.6902$$

**STEP 5: Compute Output Error**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.8 - 0.6902)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.1098)^2 = 0.006029$$

**STEP 6: Compute Output Delta ($\delta_{out}$)**

Using $\delta_{out} = (t - \hat{y}) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$:

$$\delta_{out} = (0.8 - 0.6902) \cdot (0.6902)(1 - 0.6902) = 0.1098 \cdot 0.2138 = 0.02348$$

**STEP 7: Update Weights from Hidden to Output**

$$\Delta w_{31} = \eta \cdot \delta_{out} \cdot h_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.02348 \cdot 0.5572 = 0.006541$$
$$\Delta w_{32} = \eta \cdot \delta_{out} \cdot h_2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.02348 \cdot 0.6201 = 0.007281$$
$$\Delta b_3 = \eta \cdot \delta_{out} = 0.5 \cdot 0.02348 = 0.01174$$

Updated weights:

$$w_{31}^{new} = 0.5 + 0.006541 = 0.506541$$
$$w_{32}^{new} = 0.6 + 0.007281 = 0.607281$$
$$b_3^{new} = 0.15 + 0.01174 = 0.16174$$

**STEP 8: Compute Hidden Layer Deltas**

Using $\delta_{h_i} = h_i(1 - h_i) \cdot w_{3i} \cdot \delta_{out}$:

$$\delta_{h1} = (0.5572)(1 - 0.5572) \cdot (0.506541) \cdot (0.02348) = 0.2467 \cdot 0.506541 \cdot 0.02348 = 0.002934$$
$$\delta_{h2} = (0.6201)(1 - 0.6201) \cdot (0.607281) \cdot (0.02348) = 0.2356 \cdot 0.607281 \cdot 0.02348 = 0.003359$$

**STEP 9: Update Weights from Input to Hidden**

For $w_{11}$:

$$\Delta w_{11} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h1} \cdot x_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.002934 \cdot 0.5 = 0.0007335$$

For $w_{21}$:

$$\Delta w_{21} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h1} \cdot x_2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.002934 \cdot 0.3 = 0.0004401$$

For $w_{12}$:

$$\Delta w_{12} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h2} \cdot x_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.003359 \cdot 0.5 = 0.0008398$$

For $w_{22}$:

$$\Delta w_{22} = \eta \cdot \delta_{h2} \cdot x_2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.003359 \cdot 0.3 = 0.0005039$$

Updated bias values:

$$\Delta b_1 = \eta \cdot \delta_{h1} = 0.5 \cdot 0.002934 = 0.001467$$
$$\Delta b_2 = \eta \cdot \delta_{h2} = 0.5 \cdot 0.003359 = 0.001680$$

**STEP 10: Final Updated Weight Matrix**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{11}^{new} &= 0.2 + 0.0007335 = 0.2007335 \\
w_{12}^{new} &= 0.4 + 0.0008398 = 0.4008398 \\
w_{21}^{new} &= 0.1 + 0.0004401 = 0.1004401 \\
w_{22}^{new} &= 0.3 + 0.0005039 = 0.3005039 \\
b_1^{new} &= 0.1 + 0.001467 = 0.101467 \\
b_2^{new} &= 0.2 + 0.001680 = 0.201680
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 PROBLEM TYPE 3: Activation Function Derivative Problem

### Problem Statement
For a neuron with net input $net = 1.5$:

**(a)** Compute sigmoid output and its derivative.
**(b)** Compute tanh output and its derivative.
**(c)** Compute ReLU output and its derivative.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Sigmoid**

$$\sigma(1.5) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2231} = 0.8176$$

$$\sigma'(1.5) = \sigma(1.5) \cdot (1 - \sigma(1.5)) = 0.8176 \cdot (1 - 0.8176) = 0.8176 \cdot 0.1824 = 0.1491$$

**Part (b): Tanh**

$$\tanh(1.5) = \frac{e^{1.5} - e^{-1.5}}{e^{1.5} + e^{-1.5}} = \frac{4.4817 - 0.2231}{4.4817 + 0.2231} = \frac{4.2586}{4.7048} = 0.9051$$

$$\tanh'(1.5) = 1 - \tanh^2(1.5) = 1 - (0.9051)^2 = 1 - 0.8192 = 0.1808$$

**Part (c): ReLU**

$$f(1.5) = \max(0, 1.5) = 1.5$$

$$f'(1.5) = 1 \quad (\text{since } 1.5 > 0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that for $net = 1.5$, the sigmoid derivative is very small (0.1491). This is the **vanishing gradient problem** — a key reason ReLU is preferred in deep networks!

---

## 3.4 PROBLEM TYPE 4: Gradient Descent Convergence

### Problem Statement
Minimize $E(w) = (w - 3)^2 + 2$ using gradient descent starting from $w^{(0)} = 0$ with $\eta = 0.1$. Perform 5 iterations.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**STEP 1: Compute Gradient**

$$\frac{dE}{dw} = 2(w - 3)$$

**STEP 2: Iteration Updates using $w := w - \eta \frac{dE}{dw}$**

**Iteration 1:**

$$w^{(1)} = 0 - 0.1 \cdot 2(0 - 3) = 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-6) = 0.6$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$w^{(2)} = 0.6 - 0.1 \cdot 2(0.6 - 3) = 0.6 - 0.1 \cdot (-4.8) = 0.6 + 0.48 = 1.08$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$w^{(3)} = 1.08 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.08 - 3) = 1.08 - 0.1 \cdot (-3.84) = 1.08 + 0.384 = 1.464$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$w^{(4)} = 1.464 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.464 - 3) = 1.464 + 0.3072 = 1.7712$$

**Iteration 5:**

$$w^{(5)} = 1.7712 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.7712 - 3) = 1.7712 + 0.24576 = 2.01696$$

| Iteration | $w$ | $E(w)$ |
|-----------|-----|--------|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 7.0000 |
| 1 | 0.6000 | 3.8400 |
| 2 | 1.0800 | 2.0736 |
| 3 | 1.4640 | 1.1824 |
| 4 | 1.7712 | 0.7567 |
| 5 | 2.0170 | 0.5336 |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe convergence toward $w^* = 3$ where minimum error $E_{min} = 2$.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Backpropagation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Perceptron Learning Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start] --> P2[Initialize w, b, alpha, theta]
    P2 --> P3[For each epoch]
    P3 --> P4[For each training sample x, t]
    P4 --> P5[Compute net = sum w_i x_i + b]
    P5 --> P6[Apply activation: y = f net]
    P6 --> P7{t equals y_hat?}
    P7 -- No --> P8[Update weights: w_i = w_i + alpha times t minus y_hat times x_i]
    P8 --> P9[Update bias: b = b + alpha times t minus y_hat]
    P9 --> P4
    P7 -- Yes --> P4
    P4 --> P10[Check convergence]
    P10 --> P11{All correct?}
    P11 -- No --> P3
    P11 -- Yes --> P12[Stop Training]
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P12 fill:#d4f4dd
    style P7 fill:#fff4cc
    style P11 fill:#fff4cc
```
