# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer questions worth **3 marks** each, typical of KTU Part A. Answers should be concise (around 80–120 words) and definition-focused.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **pooling** in a convolutional neural network. List any **two advantages** of using pooling layers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pooling is a downsampling operation in CNNs that reduces the spatial dimensions (height and width) of feature maps while preserving the depth. A pooling window of size $F \times F$ slides over the input with a given stride and computes a summary statistic (e.g., max, average) for each region.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Dimensionality reduction:** Significantly reduces the number of activations and parameters, lowering memory and compute cost (e.g., $2 \times 2$ pooling with stride 2 reduces size by $75\%$).
2. **Translation invariance:** Makes the network robust to small shifts in the position of features within the input image.
3. *(Bonus, if asked)*: Provides an enlarged receptive field for deeper layers, enabling them to capture global context.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two advantages, well-explained.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **max pooling** and **average pooling** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Max Pooling** | **Average Pooling** |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Selects the **largest** value in each window | Computes the **mean** of all values in each window |
| Formula | $y = \max(x_1, \ldots, x_{F^2})$ | $y = \frac{1}{F^2}\sum x_i$ |
| Output | Preserves strong activations / peaks | Smooths the feature map |
| Use case | Hidden layers (VGG, AlexNet) | Final aggregation (GoogLeNet, MobileNet) |

**Example** on $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$: Max pooling returns $9$; Average pooling returns $3.75$.

*(Award 1 mark for the difference table, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions are worth **14 marks** with internal choice (i.e., students answer either OR). Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** Explain the **pooling operation** in a Convolutional Neural Network in detail.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(i) Discuss the need for pooling in CNNs.** What problems does it solve?
**(ii) Explain the working of max pooling and average pooling with a $4 \times 4$ numerical example for each.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Need for pooling (3 marks):**
Pooling layers are essential in CNNs for the following reasons:
- **Reduces spatial dimensions** of feature maps, decreasing the number of parameters in subsequent dense layers and computational cost (FLOPs).
- **Enlarges the effective receptive field** of deeper neurons, allowing them to capture global context from the original image.
- **Provides approximate translation invariance**, making the network robust to small shifts/rotations in the input.
- **Acts as a regularizer**, reducing overfitting by preventing the network from memorizing exact spatial locations.
- **Enables hierarchical feature abstraction**, where deeper layers learn more abstract, semantically meaningful patterns.

**(ii) Max pooling example (2 marks):**
Given input $X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 9 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, with $F = 2, S = 2$:

$$Y_{max} = \begin{bmatrix} \max(1,3,2,9) & \max(2,1,1,1) \\ \max(1,3,5,6) & \max(2,3,1,2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Average pooling example (2 marks):**

$$Y_{avg} = \begin{bmatrix} (1+3+2+9)/4 & (2+1+1+1)/4 \\ (1+3+5+6)/4 & (2+3+1+2)/4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.75 & 1.25 \\ 3.75 & 2.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(i) What is Global Average Pooling (GAP)? How does it differ from standard pooling?**
**(ii) Explain the backpropagation algorithm through a max pooling layer with a worked example.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Global Average Pooling (3 marks):**
Global Average Pooling (GAP) is a special pooling operation introduced in **GoogLeNet (Szegedy et al., 2014)** where the window size equals the entire spatial dimension of the input feature map, i.e., $F = H_{in} = W_{in}$. For an input of size $H \times W \times D$, GAP produces an output of size $1 \times 1 \times D$, computed as:

$$y_c = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} x_{i,j,c}$$

**Differences from standard pooling:**

| **Aspect** | **Standard Pooling** | **Global Average Pooling** |
|---|---|---|
| Window size | Small (e.g., $2 \times 2$) | Entire feature map ($H \times W$) |
| Output shape | Reduced spatial size | $1 \times 1 \times D$ |
| Purpose | Mid-network downsampling | Replace FC layers, reduce parameters |

GAP drastically reduces parameters and improves the network's robustness to spatial translations of the input.

**(ii) Backpropagation through max pooling (4 marks):**
Since max pooling has **no learnable parameters**, the backward pass only routes the gradient from each output cell back to **the single input cell that produced the maximum** in the forward pass. A "mask" is stored during the forward pass recording the argmax position.

**Worked example:**
Forward: $X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 9 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \rightarrow Y = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ (with $F = 2, S = 2$)

Argmax mask $M$:
- Top-left window max was at $(2,2)$ with value $9$
- Top-right window max was at $(1,3)$ with value $2$
- Bottom-left window max was at $(4,1)$ with value $6$
- Bottom-right window max was at $(3,4)$ with value $3$

Given upstream gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, the input gradient becomes:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial X} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The gradient is **sparse** — only the winning position in each window receives a non-zero gradient, and all other positions receive zero.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** Discuss the various **types of pooling operations** used in CNNs.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(i) Classify the major types of pooling operations and explain any three in detail.**
**(ii) Compare max pooling and L2 pooling mathematically. What is the effect of $p$ in Lp pooling?**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Types of pooling (4 marks):**

1. **Max Pooling:** Selects the maximum value in each window. $y = \max_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}} x_{p,q}$. Preserves the strongest detected feature. Used in AlexNet, VGG, ResNet.

2. **Average (Mean) Pooling:** Computes the mean of values in each window. $y = \frac{1}{F^2} \sum x$. Smooths the feature map; used in LeNet, GoogLeNet's auxiliary classifiers.

3. **Sum Pooling:** Computes the sum of values in each window. $y = \sum x$. Functionally equivalent to average pooling without normalization.

4. **L2 (RMS) Pooling:** Computes the root-mean-square. $y = \sqrt{\frac{1}{F^2} \sum x^2}$. Penalizes large activations less than max pooling, gives a stable aggregate.

5. **Lp Pooling (Generalized):** $y = \left( \frac{1}{F^2} \sum x_i^p \right)^{1/p}$. With $p \to \infty$, it becomes max pooling; with $p = 1$, it becomes average pooling.

6. **Global Pooling:** Window covers the entire feature map. Produces $1 \times 1 \times D$ output. Includes Global Average Pooling (GAP) and Global Max Pooling (GMP).

7. **Stochastic Pooling:** Probabilistically selects one activation per window, weighted by softmax probabilities of activations. Introduces regularization through randomness (Zeiler & Fergus, 2013).

8. **Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP):** Uses multiple window sizes in parallel to produce fixed-length output regardless of input image size.

**(ii) Max vs L2 pooling (3 marks):**

Max pooling:
$$y_{max} = \max_{i \in \mathcal{R}} x_i$$

L2 pooling:
$$y_{L2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{F^2} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{R}} x_i^2}$$

**Effect of $p$ in Lp pooling:**
- $p = 1$: $y = \frac{1}{F^2} \sum x_i$ → **Average pooling**
- $p = 2$: $y = \sqrt{\frac{1}{F^2} \sum x_i^2}$ → **L2 (RMS) pooling**
- $p \to \infty$: $y = \max x_i$ → **Max pooling**

Larger $p$ values emphasize **peaks**; smaller $p$ values emphasize the **mean** behavior. Intermediate values of $p$ provide a tunable trade-off.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(i) Derive the output dimensions when a $32 \times 32 \times 16$ feature map is passed through a $3 \times 3$ max pooling layer with stride 2. Also compute the number of parameters.**
**(ii) Explain how overlap pooling differs from standard pooling. Mention its advantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dimension derivation (4 marks):**

Given: $W_{in} = H_{in} = 32$, $D_{in} = 16$, $F = 3$, $S = 2$.

Apply the formula:

$$W_{out} = \frac{W_{in} - F}{S} + 1 = \frac{32 - 3}{2} + 1 = \frac{29}{2} + 1 = 14.5 + 1 = 15.5$$

Applying the floor function (standard practice):

$$W_{out} = H_{out} = \lfloor 15.5 \rfloor = 15$$

Depth is preserved:

$$D_{out} = D_{in} = 16$$

**Output feature map size: $15 \times 15 \times 16$**

**Number of parameters:**
Pooling layers have **zero learnable parameters** — they perform fixed mathematical operations (max, mean, etc.) and have no weights or biases. The only memory overhead is the **argmax mask** stored during the forward pass for backpropagation, which is not a "parameter" in the optimization sense.

$$\text{Number of parameters} = \boxed{0}$$

**(ii) Overlapping pooling (3 marks):**
Standard (non-overlapping) pooling uses $S = F$, ensuring the windows tile the input without overlap. **Overlapping pooling** uses $S < F$, so adjacent windows share some input pixels.

**Example:** $F = 3, S = 2$ on a $6 \times 6$ input gives a $2 \times 2$ output (verified earlier). The windows overlap on a $1$-pixel-wide region.

**Advantages of overlapping pooling:**
1. **Reduced top-1 and top-5 error rates** (observed by Krizhevsky et al. in AlexNet, 2012 — overlap pooling reduced top-1 error by $\approx 0.4\%$).
2. **Smoother feature transitions** between adjacent windows.
3. **Slight implicit regularization** through the extra non-linearity of overlapping receptive fields.
4. **Better feature coverage** at object boundaries, where information is shared between windows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Pooling questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the depth preservation rule:** A common error is to write that pooling changes the number of channels. It does **not** — pooling operates channel-wise. *(−1 to −2 marks per question where this is asked.)*
> 
> 2. **Incorrect output dimension formula:** Some students write $W_{out} = W_{in}/F$ instead of $(W_{in} - F)/S + 1$. Both are wrong unless $S = F$ **and** the input is perfectly divisible. Use the correct formula and apply the floor function when needed. *(−1 to −2 marks.)*
> 
> 3. **Confusing max pooling's backward pass with average pooling's:** Max pooling routes gradient to **one position only**; average pooling **distributes** it equally. Mixing these up loses heavy marks in backpropagation sub-questions. *(−3 to −4 marks in 14-mark questions.)*
> 
> 4. **Saying pooling has "very few parameters" instead of "zero parameters":** Pooling layers have **exactly zero** learnable parameters. The word "few" is imprecise and may lose 1 mark. *(−1 mark.)*
> 
> 5. **Not drawing the argmax mask in backprop questions:** Examiners expect to see the mask explicitly. Always state which input position won the max in each window before writing the gradient. *(−2 marks.)*
> 
> 6. **Ignoring floor/ceil conventions for non-divisible sizes:** When $W_{in} = 7$, $F = 3$, $S = 2$, the output is $\lfloor (7-3)/2 + 1 \rfloor = 3$. State the floor explicitly. *(−1 mark.)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pooling (Module 4, PEBMT525)**
> 
> - **Definition:** Pooling is a downsampling operation that reduces the spatial size of feature maps while preserving depth. It has **zero learnable parameters**.
> 
> - **Output dimensions:** $W_{out} = (W_{in} - F)/S + 1$; same for $H_{out}$. **Depth is unchanged.**
> 
> - **Max pooling:** Selects the maximum value in each window. Best for preserving strong feature activations. Most common in hidden layers.
> 
> - **Average pooling:** Computes the mean of values in each window. Smoother output. Used in global pooling and older architectures (LeNet).
> 
> - **L2 pooling:** Root-mean-square of values. Intermediate between max and average.
> 
> - **Lp pooling:** $y = \left(\frac{1}{F^2}\sum x_i^p\right)^{1/p}$ — $p=1$ is average, $p=\infty$ is max.
> 
> - **Global pooling:** Window covers the entire feature map; output is $1 \times 1 \times D$. Used to replace dense layers (GoogLeNet, MobileNet).
> 
> - **Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP):** Multi-scale pooling for variable-size inputs.
> 
> - **Stochastic pooling:** Random selection weighted by softmax probabilities. Adds regularization.
> 
> - **Overlapping pooling:** $S < F$ causes adjacent windows to share input pixels. Reduces error (AlexNet finding).
> 
> - **Max pooling backprop:** Gradient flows **only to the argmax position** in each window. Sparse gradient.
> 
> - **Average pooling backprop:** Gradient is **divided equally** among all $F^2$ positions in the window. Dense gradient.
> 
> - **No padding (typically):** Pooling layers rarely use padding; they operate in "valid" mode. Non-divisible input sizes are handled by floor or by asymmetric padding.
> 
> - **Modern trend:** Many newer architectures (ResNet, ConvNeXt) use **strided convolution** instead of explicit max pooling, but pooling remains a syllabus-essential concept.
> 
> - **Engineering role:** Pooling reduces **FLOPs, memory, and overfitting**, while expanding the **receptive field** and providing **translation invariance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ReLU Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ReLU Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) Layer** is a fundamental neural network layer that applies the element-wise activation function $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ to its input tensor. Formally defined by Nair and Hinton (2010), the ReLU function is a piecewise linear, non-saturating activation that has become the **default activation function** for hidden layers in modern deep neural networks, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Feedforward Networks.

Mathematically, the ReLU activation is expressed as:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x, & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0, & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ReLU layer belongs to the family of **non-saturating activation functions**. Unlike sigmoid or tanh, ReLU does not squash large positive/negative values, which is the primary reason it mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Sigmoid/Tanh:** ReLU is **non-differentiable at $x = 0$**, but in practice, deep learning frameworks treat the sub-gradient at zero as either 0 or 1 without affecting convergence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **water tap with a one-way valve**:
- If water pressure (input) is **positive** → water flows through in proportion to the pressure (output equals input).
- If water pressure is **zero or negative** → the valve shuts off completely, and nothing flows (output is zero).

This "pass-through if positive, block if negative" behavior is exactly how ReLU works. It's a **filter that lets positive signals pass unchanged but completely silences negative signals**, mimicking how biological neurons behave — they either fire (positive activation) or remain silent (zero output).

## 1.3 Physical & Design Properties

- **Sparsity:** ReLU naturally induces **sparse activations** (many neurons output exactly 0), which improves computational efficiency.
- **Range:** Output domain is $[0, \infty)$.
- **Computational Cost:** Only a thresholding operation — **$O(1)$ per element**, dramatically cheaper than $\exp(\cdot)$ in sigmoid.
- **Biological Plausibility:** Closely resembles the **half-wave rectification** observed in real biological neurons.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The ReLU layer operates on a tensor (input from previous layer) through these stages:

1. **Linear Pre-Activation Input:** The previous fully-connected or convolutional layer produces a pre-activation value $z = Wx + b$, where $W$ is the weight matrix, $x$ is the input, and $b$ is the bias.
2. **Element-wise Thresholding:** Each scalar $z_i$ in the pre-activation tensor is passed individually through the ReLU function.
3. **Output Generation:** The output $a_i = \max(0, z_i)$ is forwarded to the next layer.
4. **Gradient Propagation (Backward Pass):** The derivative is computed and multiplied (chain rule) with upstream gradients to update weights.

## 2.2 Mathematical Definition of the Derivative

The derivative of ReLU is the **Heaviside step function**:

$$f'(z) = \frac{d}{dz}\max(0, z) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } z > 0 \\ 0, & \text{if } z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention at $z=0$:** In KTU examinations, the sub-gradient is conventionally taken as either 0 or 1. Both are accepted as valid answers.

## 2.3 Why "Why" and "How" — Engineering Justification

| Aspect | Why ReLU Works | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Derivative is constant (1) for $z>0$, so gradients don't shrink exponentially | Maintains strong error signals across many layers |
| **Computational Speed** | No expensive exponentials | Single comparison + multiplication per neuron |
| **Sparse Representations** | Negative inputs map to 0 | Yields biologically plausible, efficient codes |
| **Convergence** | Empirically 6x faster than tanh (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) | Linear region allows aggressive learning rates |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of pipe characters inside tables to avoid syntax breakage.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Pass | $a = \max(0, z)$ | Activation output |
| Forward Variant | $a = \max(0, z) = z \cdot \mathbb{1}[z>0]$ | Indicator representation |
| Derivative | $f'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z>0]$ | Backpropagation gradient |
| Sub-gradient at 0 | $f'(0) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Convention for KTU exams |
| Leaky ReLU | $f(z) = \max(\alpha z, z),\ \alpha = 0.01$ | Prevents dead neurons |
| Parametric ReLU | $f(z) = \max(\alpha z, z),\ \alpha \text{ learnable}$ | Adaptive slope |
| ELU | $f(z) = z\ \text{if}\ z>0,\ \alpha(e^{z}-1)\ \text{otherwise}$ | Smooth negative region |
| Range | $a \in [0, \infty)$ | Output bounds |
| Dead Neuron Count | $N_{\text{dead}} = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[z_i \leq 0]$ | Diagnostic metric |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The ReLU layer is the **de-facto standard activation** in:
- **AlexNet (2012):** Sparked the deep learning revolution.
- **VGG, ResNet, Inception:** All major CNN backbones.
- **Transformers (Self-Attention):** Used in feedforward sub-blocks of BERT, GPT.
- **GANs and Autoencoders:** Hidden layer activation of choice.

In real-time embedded systems (KTU-affiliated IoT and edge AI applications), ReLU is preferred over sigmoid because of its **minimal memory footprint** and **deterministic computation time** — critical for FPGA/ASIC deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ReLU Function from First Principles

**Step 1: Start from the ReLU definition.**

We are given the piecewise function:

$$f(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Express using the indicator function.**

Define the Heaviside step function as:

$$H(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

Using $H(z)$, we can compactly write ReLU as:

$$f(z) = z \cdot H(z)$$

**Step 3: Derive the first derivative with respect to $z$.**

Applying the product rule:

$$\frac{df}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}[z \cdot H(z)] = H(z) + z \cdot \frac{dH}{dz}$$

Since $H(z)$ is a step function, its derivative is the **Dirac delta** $\delta(z)$:

$$\frac{df}{dz} = H(z) + z \cdot \delta(z)$$

At $z \neq 0$, the second term vanishes because $z = 0$ only at the origin, so:

$$\frac{df}{dz} = H(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Establish the sub-gradient convention at $z=0$.**

The function is non-differentiable strictly at $z=0$. By taking the sub-differential:

$$\partial f(0) = \{c \mid 0 \leq c \leq 1\}$$

Therefore, in implementation:

$$f'(0) \in [0, 1]$$

Most frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) set $f'(0) = 0$.

## 3.2 Backpropagation Through a ReLU Layer — Worked Derivation

Consider a simple network:

$$L = \text{Loss}\left(y, \hat{y}\right), \quad \hat{y} = f(z), \quad z = Wx + b$$

**Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$.

**Step 1: Chain Rule for Output Layer.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot f'(z)$$

**Step 2: Substitute the ReLU derivative.**

$$f'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$$

So:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$$

**Step 3: Compute weight gradient.**

Since $z = Wx + b$, applying chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \mathbb{1}[z > 0] \cdot x^{\top}$$

**Step 4: Compute bias gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial b} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Whenever $z \leq 0$, the gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = 0$. This means the corresponding neuron **stops learning** — known as the **"Dying ReLU Problem."**

## 3.3 Numerical Walkthrough — ReLU Forward Pass

Given input vector $\mathbf{x} = [-2.5,\ 0.0,\ 1.7,\ -0.3,\ 3.2]$:

| Index $i$ | Input $z_i$ | ReLU Output $a_i = \max(0, z_i)$ | Active? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-2.5$ | $0$ | No |
| 2 | $0.0$ | $0$ | No (boundary) |
| 3 | $1.7$ | $1.7$ | Yes |
| 4 | $-0.3$ | $0$ | No |
| 5 | $3.2$ | $3.2$ | Yes |

**Sparsity Achieved:** 3 out of 5 neurons ($60\%$) are inactive — demonstrating the **sparse representation** property.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing ReLU Variants

| Variant | Forward Formula | Derivative | Dead Neuron Issue? |
|---|---|---|---|
| ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $\mathbb{1}[z>0]$ | Yes |
| Leaky ReLU | $\max(0.01z, z)$ | $0.01$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | No |
| PReLU | $\max(\alpha z, z)$, $\alpha$ learnable | $\alpha$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | No |
| ELU | $z$ if $z>0$; $\alpha(e^{z}-1)$ otherwise | $1$ if $z>0$; $\alpha e^{z}$ otherwise | No |
| GELU | $z \cdot \Phi(z)$ | $\Phi(z) + z\phi(z)$ | No |

Where $\Phi$ is the Gaussian CDF and $\phi$ is the Gaussian PDF.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ReLU Activation Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a ReLU layer within a deep network, mapping its interactions with the upstream linear layer and the downstream loss function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Map x] --> B[Linear Layer: z = Wx + b]
    B --> C[Pre-activation Tensor z]
    C --> D{ReLU Threshold: z greater than 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Pass-through: a = z]
    D -- No --> F[Suppress: a = 0]
    E --> G[Output Activations a]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Next Layer / Loss Function]
    H --> I[Compute Gradient dL_dz]
    I --> J{Is z greater than 0?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Pass gradient: dL_dz times 1]
    J -- No --> L[Block gradient: dL_dz times 0]
    K --> M[Update Weights: dW = dL_dz times x transpose]
    L --> N[Weights frozen for this neuron]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNN with ReLU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input Image 224x224x3] --> CN1[Conv2D Layer]
    CN1 --> RL1[ReLU Layer]
    RL1 --> PL1[MaxPooling 2x2]
    PL1 --> CN2[Conv2D Layer]
    CN2 --> RL2[ReLU Layer]
    RL2 --> PL2[MaxPooling 2x2]
    PL2 --> FL1[Flatten Vector]
    FL1 --> FC1[Dense Layer 128 units]
    FC1 --> RL3[ReLU Layer]
    RL3 --> DR1[Dropout 0.5]
    DR1 --> FC2[Dense Softmax 10 classes]
    FC2 --> OUT1[Class Probabilities]
```

## 4.3 Decision Boundary Schematic for ReLU vs Sigmoid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sigmoid_Region[Sigmoid Activation]
        S1[Input z] --> S2[Squash to 0 comma 1]
        S2 --> S3[Vanishing gradient for large z]
    end
    subgraph ReLU_Region[ReLU Activation]
        R1[Input z] --> R2{Compare z with 0}
        R2 -- z greater than 0 --> R3[Linear: gradient = 1]
        R2 -- z less than 0 --> R4[Zero: gradient = 0]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** The sigmoid's curve asymptotically flattens on both sides (saturation), while ReLU maintains a constant slope of 1 for all positive inputs — this is the geometric reason it preserves gradient magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, Set B]**

**Q1. Define the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function. State its mathematical form and the range of its output.**

**Model Answer:**

The Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is a non-linear, piecewise linear activation function used in hidden layers of deep neural networks. It is defined as:

$$f(z) = \max(0, z)$$

- **Output Range:** $[0, \infty)$
- **Differentiability:** Differentiable everywhere except at $z = 0$.
- **Derivative:** $f'(z) = 1$ for $z > 0$ and $0$ for $z \leq 0$.
- **Key Property:** Non-saturating for positive inputs, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

> *Valuation Tip: A complete answer must include the piecewise form, range, and at least one advantage. Missing the range costs 1 mark.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary, Set A]**

**Q2. What is the "Dying ReLU" problem? Suggest one modification to overcome it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Dying ReLU problem** occurs when a ReLU neuron receives a large negative bias update, causing its pre-activation $z$ to remain $\leq 0$ for all training examples. Consequently, the gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = 0$ permanently, and the neuron stops learning — it is "dead."

**Modification:** Use **Leaky ReLU**, defined as:

$$f(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z > 0 \\ \alpha z, & z \leq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{where } \alpha = 0.01$$

This ensures a small non-zero gradient for negative inputs, preventing neurons from dying.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the ReLU activation function with respect to its input $z$. Clearly state the sub-gradient convention used at $z = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the forward and backward propagation of signals through a ReLU layer in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). Discuss why ReLU is preferred over sigmoid in deep architectures. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Start with the definition $f(z) = \max(0, z)$.

**Step 2:** For $z > 0$: $f(z) = z \Rightarrow f'(z) = 1$.

**Step 3:** For $z \leq 0$: $f(z) = 0 \Rightarrow f'(z) = 0$.

**Step 4:** The function is non-differentiable at $z=0$. By the sub-differential definition:

$$\partial f(0) = \{c \in \mathbb{R} \mid 0 \leq c \leq 1\}$$

Standard convention in deep learning frameworks: $f'(0) = 0$ (PyTorch, TensorFlow default).

**Final Result:**

$$f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

The CNN block flow is: `Input → Conv2D → ReLU → Pooling → ... → Fully Connected → Softmax`.

**Forward Pass:** For each element $z_{i,j,k}$ in the pre-activation feature map, the ReLU outputs $a_{i,j,k} = \max(0, z_{i,j,k})$.

**Backward Pass:** The upstream gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial a}$ is element-wise multiplied by $f'(z)$, producing a sparse gradient mask where inactive neurons receive zero gradient.

**Why ReLU over Sigmoid in Deep Architectures:**

| Property | ReLU | Sigmoid |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient for $z > 0$ | Constant = 1 | Decays exponentially |
| Computational cost | Threshold ($\approx$ 1 ns) | Exponential (costly) |
| Sparsity | Yes | No |
| Vanishing gradient | Resolved | Severe |

**Conclusion:** ReLU enables the training of very deep networks (e.g., ResNet-152) that would be untrainable with sigmoid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Compare ReLU, Leaky ReLU, Parametric ReLU (PReLU), and ELU activation functions using a comparison matrix. Show the derivative of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hidden layer in a neural network produces the pre-activation vector $z = [-1.5, 0.2, -3.0, 4.5, 0.0, -0.7]$ after a linear transformation. Compute:
   (i) The ReLU output vector.
   (ii) The number of "dead" neurons.
   (iii) The output vector if Leaky ReLU with $\alpha = 0.1$ is used instead. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Comparison table already provided in Section 3.4. Student should reproduce with explicit formulas:

- ReLU: $f(z) = \max(0, z)$, $f'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z>0]$
- Leaky ReLU: $f(z) = \max(0.1z, z)$, $f'(z) = 0.1$ if $z \leq 0$, else $1$
- PReLU: $f(z) = \max(\alpha z, z)$ with learnable $\alpha$, derivative: $\alpha$ if $z<0$, else $1$
- ELU: $f(z) = z$ if $z>0$, else $\alpha(e^z - 1)$; derivative: $1$ if $z>0$, else $\alpha e^z$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) ReLU output:**

$$a = \max(0, z) = [0,\ 0.2,\ 0,\ 4.5,\ 0,\ 0]$$

**(ii) Dead neurons:** Count of indices where $z_i \leq 0$:

$N_{\text{dead}} = 5$ (indices 1, 3, 5, 6, and boundary at index 5)

**(iii) Leaky ReLU output with $\alpha = 0.1$:**

For negative $z$: $a_i = 0.1 \times z_i$.

$$a_{\text{leaky}} = [0.1 \times (-1.5),\ 0.2,\ 0.1 \times (-3.0),\ 4.5,\ 0,\ 0.1 \times (-0.7)]$$

$$a_{\text{leaky}} = [-0.15,\ 0.2,\ -0.30,\ 4.5,\ 0.0,\ -0.07]$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the range:** Many students write $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ but omit that the output is in $[0, \infty)$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Sub-gradient oversight:** At $z = 0$, students often incorrectly write a single value (either 0 or 1) without acknowledging the **non-differentiability** — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing ReLU with Softplus:** Softplus is $f(z) = \ln(1 + e^z)$, which is *smooth*. Conflating these loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Dying ReLU:** When asked about disadvantages, students only mention "non-zero-centered output" but forget the dying neuron phenomenon — costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Arithmetic mistakes in numericals:** In Question B(b), students forget to handle $z = 0$ at the boundary — costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: ReLU Layer**

- **Definition:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ — piecewise linear, non-saturating.
- **Range:** $[0, \infty)$ (not zero-centered).
- **Derivative:** $1$ for $z > 0$; $0$ for $z \leq 0$; sub-gradient at 0 is in $[0, 1]$.
- **Vanishing Gradient:** Solved for positive inputs; gradients remain $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
- **Computational Cost:** $O(1)$ per neuron — fastest among all activation functions.
- **Sparsity:** Induces natural sparse representations; typical 50% activation rate in practice.
- **Dying ReLU:** Neurons stuck at $a = 0$ permanently — fixed by Leaky ReLU / PReLU / ELU.
- **Not Zero-Centered:** Causes zig-zag dynamics in gradient descent (use BatchNorm to mitigate).
- **Variants to Remember:** Leaky ReLU ($\alpha = 0.01$), PReLU (learnable $\alpha$), ELU, GELU, Swish.
- **Where Used:** All modern CNN backbones (ResNet, VGG, Inception); Transformer FFN blocks; GAN hidden layers.
- **Derivative Trick:** $f'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$ — memorise the indicator form for fast exam recall.
- **Key Equation for Backprop:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \mathbb{1}[z > 0] \cdot x^{\top}$.
- **He Initialization Pairing:** ReLU is optimally paired with **He initialization** ($W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2/n)$), not Xavier.
- **Learning Rate:** ReLU tolerates higher learning rates ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$) compared to sigmoid.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of CNN (Basic Concepts Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure of CNN (Basic Concepts Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a specialized class of deep feed-forward neural networks designed to process data that has a known grid-like topology, such as images (2D grid of pixels) or time-series signals (1D grid). It uses a mathematical operation called **convolution** in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of its layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A CNN is a hierarchical, multi-layer feed-forward neural network composed of three primary building blocks — **Convolutional Layers**, **Pooling (Sub-sampling) Layers**, and **Fully Connected Layers** — arranged in a sequential, learnable architecture that automatically extracts spatial feature hierarchies from input data.

The fundamental building blocks of a CNN architecture are:

1. **Convolutional Layer (CONV):** Performs the convolution operation using learnable filters (kernels).
2. **Pooling / Sub-sampling Layer (POOL):** Performs downsampling to reduce spatial dimensionality.
3. **Fully Connected Layer (FC):** Acts as a classical MLP classifier at the end of the network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Visual System

Imagine a child learning to recognize a cat:

- The **retina** captures raw light patterns.
- The **primary visual cortex (V1)** detects simple features like **edges, lines, and oriented gradients**.
- Higher visual areas (**V2, V4, IT cortex**) combine these simple features into **shapes, textures, and parts** (ears, eyes, whiskers).
- The final recognition area assembles everything into a **complete concept**: "this is a cat."

A CNN mimics this exact hierarchy:

| Biological Visual Pathway | CNN Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Photoreceptors (retina) | Input image (pixel matrix) |
| V1 cortex (edges) | Early convolutional layers (low-level features) |
| V2/V4 cortex (textures, parts) | Middle convolutional layers (mid-level features) |
| IT cortex (whole object) | Deep convolutional layers (high-level features) |
| Brain's decision area | Fully connected classifier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Inspiration:** CNNs were directly inspired by the work of **Hubel and Wiesel (1959, 1962)** on the cat visual cortex, which identified two key cell types: **Simple Cells** (respond to specific orientations in a small region — analogous to convolution filters) and **Complex Cells** (pool responses from simple cells over small neighborhoods — analogous to pooling operations).

## 1.3 Why CNNs Exist — The Problem with Traditional ANNs on Images

For a modest $64 \times 64$ RGB color image:
- Number of input pixels $= 64 \times 64 \times 3 = \mathbf{12{,}288}$ inputs.
- A traditional fully-connected ANN with even just **100** hidden neurons in the first layer would require:

$$w = 12{,}288 \times 100 = \mathbf{1{,}228{,}800} \text{ weights}$$

This explosion causes **overfitting, massive computational cost, and ignores the spatial structure** of images. CNNs solve this through two core principles:

- **Local Receptive Fields:** Neurons connect only to small patches of the previous layer.
- **Shared Weights (Parameter Sharing):** The same filter is slid across the entire image.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** A single $5 \times 5$ filter applied across an entire image uses only **25 parameters** regardless of image size. This is the **parameter efficiency** that makes CNNs revolutionary.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Structural Components of a CNN

### 2.1.1 Convolutional Layer (CONV)

- Uses a set of **learnable filters (kernels)** of small spatial extent (e.g., $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$, $7 \times 7$).
- Each filter is convolved (slid) across the input to produce a **feature map (activation map)**.
- A single layer typically contains **multiple filters**, each specialized in detecting a different feature.
- Followed by a non-linear activation function — usually **ReLU** $f(x) = \max(0, x)$.

**The 'Why':** Convolution exploits **spatial locality** (nearby pixels are correlated) and **translation equivariance** (a feature is meaningful regardless of its position).

### 2.1.2 Pooling Layer (POOL)

- Performs **downsampling** on feature maps to reduce spatial size and computational load.
- Most common type: **Max-Pooling** (takes the maximum value in a small window).
- Also used: **Average-Pooling**, **L2-Pooling**, **Global Average Pooling**.

**The 'Why':** Provides **spatial invariance** (small shifts in input do not change the output) and reduces the number of parameters in subsequent layers, combating overfitting.

### 2.1.3 Fully Connected Layer (FC)

- Standard MLP neurons where every neuron is connected to all neurons in the previous layer.
- Appears at the **end of the network** to perform high-level reasoning and final classification.
- Outputs are passed through a **Softmax** (multi-class) or **Sigmoid** (binary) function.

## 2.2 Key CNN Terminology — A Student's Mental Dictionary

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Filter / Kernel** | A small matrix of learnable weights (e.g., $3 \times 3$) that is convolved with the input to extract features. |
| **Feature Map** | The output of a filter after convolution over the entire input. One filter = one feature map. |
| **Stride ($S$)** | The step size with which the filter moves across the input. |
| **Padding ($P$)** | Zero-pixels added around the input border to control output size. Types: 'Valid' (no padding) and 'Same' (preserve size). |
| **Depth** | The number of filters used in a convolutional layer. |
| **Receptive Field** | The region of the original input that influences a particular neuron in a deeper layer. |
| **Local Receptive Field** | The small input region a single neuron "sees" in the first conv layer. |
| **Shared Weights** | The same filter weights are used at all spatial locations in a given layer. |
| **Volume** | The 3D tensor $(W \times H \times D)$ representation of data flowing through the network. |
| **Activation Map** | Synonym for feature map, emphasizing that the conv layer is followed by a non-linearity. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials** for KTU ESE. Memorize the table — questions frequently test output dimension calculation.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conv Output Width/Height** | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $W_{in}$: input size, $F$: filter size, $P$: padding, $S$: stride |
| **Pool Output Width/Height** | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W_{in} - F}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Same as above (no padding in pooling) |
| **# Parameters in a CONV Layer** | $P_{conv} = (F \times F \times D_{in} + 1) \times D_{out}$ | $D_{in}$: input depth, $D_{out}$: # filters, the **+1** is for the bias term per filter |
| **# Parameters in a FC Layer** | $P_{fc} = (N_{in} + 1) \times N_{out}$ | $N_{in}$: input neurons, $N_{out}$: output neurons, **+1** for bias |
| **Receptive Field Growth** | $RF_{l+1} = RF_{l} + (F_{l+1} - 1) \times S_{\text{product}}$ | Recursive; $S_{\text{product}}$ is the product of all prior strides |
| **ReLU Activation** | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Non-linear element-wise function |
| **Softmax (output)** | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Converts logits to a probability distribution over $K$ classes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of CNNs

- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor detection in MRI/CT scans (e.g., U-Net for segmentation).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time object detection in self-driving cars (YOLO, Faster R-CNN).
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect detection on assembly lines via surface image analysis.
- **Satellite & Remote Sensing:** Land-use classification, disaster-zone mapping.
- **Biometrics:** Face recognition, fingerprint authentication, iris scanning.
- **Agriculture:** Crop disease identification from leaf images.
- **Document Processing:** OCR, handwriting recognition, automated form processing.

> [!TIP]
> The **hierarchical feature learning** capability of CNNs is what made modern computer vision possible — they **automatically learn features** instead of relying on hand-crafted feature extractors (like HOG, SIFT, LBP used before 2012).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Dimension of a Convolutional Layer

**Statement:** Given an input volume of size $W_{in} \times H_{in} \times D_{in}$ and a filter of size $F \times F$ applied with stride $S$ and zero-padding $P$, derive the output volume size $W_{out} \times H_{out} \times D_{out}$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Understand the geometric motion of the filter:**

The filter must start such that its top-left corner aligns with the top-left of the (padded) input, and it slides until its bottom-right corner aligns with the bottom-right of the (padded) input. The total distance travelled is:

$$D_{travel} = W_{in} + 2P - F$$

**Step 2 — Count the number of valid landing positions of the filter:**

Since the filter moves $S$ pixels per step, the number of distinct positions is:

$$N_{positions} = \frac{D_{travel}}{S} + 1 = \frac{W_{in} + 2P - F}{S} + 1$$

**Step 3 — Convert to floor function for edge cases:**

If the filter does not perfectly fit, we discard the last partial position. Therefore:

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2P - F}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

Rearranging for the standard KTU form:

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 4 — Same logic for height (assume square filter):**

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 5 — Determine output depth:**

Each filter produces **one** 2D feature map. If we have $D_{out}$ filters, we stack them along the depth axis:

$$D_{out} = \text{Number of filters in the layer}$$

**Final Output Volume:**

$$\boxed{\text{Output Volume} = W_{out} \times H_{out} \times D_{out}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Trainable Parameters in a CONV Layer

**Statement:** For a convolutional layer with $D_{out}$ filters of spatial size $F \times F \times D_{in}$, derive the total parameter count $P_{conv}$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Count weights per filter:**

Each filter spans $F \times F$ in spatial dimensions and must match the full input depth $D_{in}$ (to capture all input channels):

$$W_{\text{per filter}} = F \times F \times D_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Add bias per filter:**

Each filter has **one scalar bias term** (added to the entire output feature map):

$$W_{\text{per filter with bias}} = F \times F \times D_{in} + 1$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the number of filters:**

Since we have $D_{out}$ independent filters, each with its own bias:

$$P_{conv} = (F \times F \times D_{in} + 1) \times D_{out}$$

$$\boxed{P_{conv} = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot D_{out}}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU-style numerical problem. Practice variations of this.

**Problem:** An input image of size $\mathbf{32 \times 32 \times 3}$ is passed through a convolutional layer with **10 filters** of size $\mathbf{5 \times 5}$, **stride** $S = 1$, and **padding** $P = 2$ (Same padding). Calculate the output volume dimensions and the number of trainable parameters.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the convolution output formula:**

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

Substituting values:

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{27 + 4}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 31 + 1 = 32$$

**Step 2 — Output dimensions:**

$$\text{Output Volume} = 32 \times 32 \times 10$$

(Since we used 10 filters, output depth = 10, and same padding preserved the spatial size at 32.)

**Step 3 — Calculate parameters:**

$$P_{conv} = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \times D_{out}$$

$$P_{conv} = (5 \times 5 \times 3 + 1) \times 10$$

$$P_{conv} = (75 + 1) \times 10 = 76 \times 10 = 760$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{Output Volume} = 32 \times 32 \times 10, \quad \text{Parameters} = 760}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** A fully connected layer connecting the input ($32 \times 32 \times 3 = 3072$ neurons) to the same number of 10240 output neurons would require $3072 \times 10240 \approx \mathbf{31.4 \text{ million}}$ parameters. The CNN layer uses only **760** parameters. This dramatic reduction is the central engineering advantage of CNNs.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Receptive Field Size

**Statement:** For a stack of layers with filters of size $F_l$ and strides $S_l$, derive the receptive field size at layer $L$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Start with the first layer:**

The receptive field of the first conv layer is just the filter size:

$$RF_1 = F_1$$

**Step 2 — Recursive expansion for subsequent layers:**

When the next layer's filter of size $F_2$ moves with stride $S_1$ over a region, each new neuron "sees" the previous neuron's receptive field plus extra spatial coverage:

$$RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (F_{l+1} - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l} S_i$$

**Step 3 — Iterative unfolding for a 2-layer example:**

$$RF_2 = F_1 + (F_2 - 1) \cdot S_1$$

$$RF_3 = RF_2 + (F_3 - 1) \cdot (S_1 \cdot S_2)$$

**Step 4 — General closed form:**

$$\boxed{RF_L = F_1 + \sum_{l=2}^{L}(F_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i}$$

**Interpretation:** Deeper neurons in a CNN have a **larger receptive field** — they "see" more of the original input image, which is exactly why deep CNNs can capture **global, high-level features** from small local patches.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNN Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow of data** through a standard CNN architecture. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain only plain uppercase text (per compilation safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE 32x32x3 RGB PIXEL MATRIX] --> B[CONV LAYER L1 WITH 6 FILTERS 5x5 STRIDE 1 PADDING 2]
    B --> C[RELU ACTIVATION APPLIED ELEMENT WISE]
    C --> D[MAX POOLING LAYER 2x2 WINDOW STRIDE 2]
    D --> E[CONV LAYER L2 WITH 16 FILTERS 5x5 STRIDE 1 PADDING 0]
    E --> F[RELU ACTIVATION APPLIED ELEMENT WISE]
    F --> G[MAX POOLING LAYER 2x2 WINDOW STRIDE 2]
    G --> H[FLATTEN OPERATION CONVERT 3D TO 1D VECTOR]
    H --> I[FULLY CONNECTED LAYER FC1 WITH 120 NEURONS]
    I --> J[RELU ACTIVATION APPLIED ELEMENT WISE]
    J --> K[FULLY CONNECTED LAYER FC2 WITH 84 NEURONS]
    K --> L[SOFTMAX OUTPUT LAYER WITH 10 CLASSES]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Data flows **top-to-bottom**. Notice the alternating pattern of **CONV → ReLU → POOL** blocks, which is the signature rhythm of every classic CNN. Spatial dimensions shrink while depth grows at each CONV step.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Feature Learning Diagram

This diagram visualizes the **layer-wise feature abstraction** that occurs as data moves through a CNN. It explains *what* each layer learns to detect.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[LAYER 1 LOW LEVEL FEATURES EDGES ORIENTED LINES COLOR GRADIENTS] --> P2[LAYER 2 MID LEVEL FEATURES CORNERS TEXTURES SIMPLE SHAPES]
    P2 --> P3[LAYER 3 HIGH LEVEL FEATURES OBJECT PARTS EYES WHEELS FACES]
    P3 --> P4[LAYER 4 TASK SPECIFIC FEATURES COMPLETE OBJECT CONCEPTS]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Early layers (close to the input) learn **generic, transferable features** like edges and gradients. These features are useful across many vision tasks. Deeper layers learn **task-specific features** that are highly specialized to the problem at hand.

## 4.3 Parameter Sharing Concept Diagram

This schematic illustrates how a single filter "scans" across the entire image using **shared weights**, a key efficiency mechanism of CNNs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INPUT REGION TOP LEFT 5x5 PATCH] --> F[SHARED 5x5 FILTER SAME WEIGHTS]
    A2[INPUT REGION TOP MIDDLE 5x5 PATCH] --> F
    A3[INPUT REGION CENTER 5x5 PATCH] --> F
    A4[INPUT REGION BOTTOM RIGHT 5x5 PATCH] --> F
    F --> B1[FEATURE MAP OUTPUT 1]
    F --> B2[FEATURE MAP OUTPUT 2]
    F --> B3[FEATURE MAP OUTPUT 3]
    F --> B4[FEATURE MAP OUTPUT 4]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Diagram:** The same filter (set of 25 + 1 = 26 parameters) is reused at every spatial location. This is why CNNs have **far fewer parameters** than fully connected networks of comparable depth.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define a Convolutional Neural Network. List any four advantages of CNNs over traditional fully-connected neural networks for image processing.**

**Model Answer:**

A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is a class of deep neural networks specifically designed to process grid-structured data such as images, using convolution operations in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of its layers.

**Four advantages of CNNs:**

1. **Parameter Efficiency:** Through weight sharing and local connectivity, CNNs use drastically fewer parameters than fully connected networks of comparable depth.
2. **Spatial Hierarchy Learning:** Automatically learns a hierarchy of features — from low-level edges to high-level object parts.
3. **Translation Equivariance:** Can detect features regardless of their spatial position in the image.
4. **Reduced Overfitting:** Fewer parameters and built-in regularization (via pooling) reduce the risk of overfitting on image data.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the following terms with respect to CNNs: (i) Local Receptive Field, (ii) Shared Weights, (iii) Feature Map.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Local Receptive Field:** In a CNN, each neuron in a convolutional layer is connected to only a small region (patch) of the previous layer's output rather than to all neurons. This small region is the local receptive field, which allows the network to focus on local spatial correlations.

**(ii) Shared Weights:** The same filter (set of weights) is convolved across the entire input volume. This means all neurons in a given feature map use **identical weights and biases**, dramatically reducing the number of trainable parameters.

**(iii) Feature Map:** The 2D output produced by a single filter after convolution over the input. It highlights **where in the input** a particular feature (the one the filter detects) is strongly activated.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question offers internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two fully independent alternatives — a student attempts *any one*.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the basic architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A standard CNN architecture consists of an input layer, alternating convolutional and pooling layers, followed by one or more fully connected layers, and a final output layer (typically Softmax).

**Block Diagram:**

```
INPUT IMAGE → [CONV1 + ReLU] → [POOL1] → [CONV2 + ReLU] → [POOL2] → [FLATTEN]
            → [FC1] → [FC2] → [SOFTMAX OUTPUT]
```

**Role of Each Layer:**

1. **Input Layer:** Holds the raw pixel values of the input image as a 3D volume $(H \times W \times C)$.

2. **Convolutional Layer:** Applies learnable filters that convolve across the input to extract low-level features such as edges, corners, and color gradients. Each filter produces a 2D feature map. The layer is followed by a non-linear activation, usually **ReLU**, to introduce non-linearity.

3. **Pooling Layer:** Performs downsampling (typically max-pooling) on each feature map to reduce spatial dimensions, control overfitting, and introduce a degree of translation invariance.

4. **Flatten Layer:** Converts the final 3D feature volume into a 1D vector so it can be fed into a fully connected layer.

5. **Fully Connected Layer:** Standard MLP neurons that combine the high-level features extracted by earlier layers to perform classification.

6. **Output Layer (Softmax):** Outputs a probability distribution over the $K$ target classes, $\sum_i P_i = 1$.

---

**(b)** For an input image of size $\mathbf{64 \times 64 \times 3}$, a convolutional layer uses **20 filters** of size $\mathbf{3 \times 3}$ with **stride** $S = 1$ and **valid padding** ($P = 0$). Calculate: (i) the output volume size, and (ii) the total number of trainable parameters in this layer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** $W_{in} = 64, H_{in} = 64, D_{in} = 3, F = 3, S = 1, P = 0, D_{out} = 20$

**(i) Output Volume Size:**

Using the formula:

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{64 - 3 + 0}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 61 + 1 = 62$$

$$H_{out} = 62 \text{ (same as width for square input)}$$

$$D_{out} = 20$$

$$\boxed{\text{Output Volume} = 62 \times 62 \times 20}$$

**(ii) Total Trainable Parameters:**

Using the formula:

$$P_{conv} = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \times D_{out}$$

$$P_{conv} = (3 \times 3 \times 3 + 1) \times 20 = (27 + 1) \times 20 = 28 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{P_{conv} = 560 \text{ parameters}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **local receptive fields**, **shared weights**, and **feature maps** in a CNN. Discuss how these concepts lead to parameter efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Local Receptive Field:**

In a traditional fully connected network, each neuron in one layer is connected to *every* neuron in the previous layer. In a CNN, each neuron in a convolutional layer connects only to a small, localized region of the previous layer. This region is called the **local receptive field** (e.g., a $5 \times 5$ patch). This design is biologically inspired and exploits the fact that **spatially close pixels are highly correlated**, while distant pixels are less so.

**2. Shared Weights:**

A single filter (with a fixed set of weights and one bias) is **slid across the entire input volume** to produce one feature map. This means that all neurons within a single feature map **share the exact same weights and bias**. The advantage is twofold:
- The network becomes **translation equivariant** — the same feature can be detected anywhere in the image.
- The number of trainable parameters is **drastically reduced**.

**3. Feature Map:**

A feature map is the 2D output produced by convolving one filter across the spatial extent of the input. It highlights regions of the input where a particular feature (e.g., a vertical edge) is strongly present. A convolutional layer with $D_{out}$ filters produces $D_{out}$ feature maps stacked along the depth axis.

**Parameter Efficiency — Numerical Comparison:**

Consider a $32 \times 32 \times 3$ image fed to a layer producing 32 feature maps of the same size with a $5 \times 5$ filter:

- **CNN layer parameters:** $(5 \times 5 \times 3 + 1) \times 32 = 76 \times 32 = \mathbf{2{,}432}$ parameters.
- **Equivalent fully connected layer** (input 3072 → output 3072 neurons of equal capacity): $3072 \times 3072 + 3072 \approx \mathbf{9.4 \text{ million}}$ parameters.

The CNN uses **~3,867 times fewer parameters** while achieving equivalent representational power for spatially correlated data.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast a **traditional Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** with a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** for image classification tasks. Highlight at least four key differences. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| # | Aspect | Traditional ANN | CNN |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Connectivity** | Fully connected: every neuron connects to *all* neurons in the previous layer. | Locally connected: each neuron connects to only a small local receptive field. |
| 2 | **Parameter Count** | Very high (e.g., millions for even modest images). | Dramatically low due to weight sharing and local connectivity. |
| 3 | **Spatial Structure** | Ignores spatial relationships; input is flattened into a 1D vector. | Preserves spatial topology; input is treated as a 3D volume $(H \times W \times C)$. |
| 4 | **Translation Handling** | A feature learned at one location must be relearned at another location. | **Translation equivariance** via weight sharing — a feature is detected regardless of position. |
| 5 | **Feature Engineering** | Requires hand-crafted feature extractors (HOG, SIFT) before classification. | **Automatic hierarchical feature learning** directly from raw pixels. |
| 6 | **Computational Cost** | Extremely high for large images due to dense weight matrices. | Much lower; convolution is highly optimized and parallelizable on GPUs. |
| 7 | **Typical Use Case** | Tabular data, simple classification with structured inputs. | Image, video, audio spectrograms, and other spatially correlated grid data. |

**Conclusion:** For image classification, CNNs are vastly superior to traditional ANNs in **parameter efficiency, spatial awareness, and automatic feature extraction**, making them the de-facto standard for modern computer vision tasks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" bias term** in the parameter count formula. The bias is **per filter**, not per neuron. Writing $(F \times F \times D_{in}) \times D_{out}$ instead of $(F \times F \times D_{in} + 1) \times D_{out}$ is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Valid" and "Same" padding** in numerical problems. Valid padding = $P = 0$. Same padding = $P = (F-1)/2$ to preserve spatial size. Many students write the wrong $P$ value.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ in the output dimension formula. When $W_{in} - F + 2P$ is not divisible by $S$, the result is truncated — students often write a non-integer answer.
>
> 4. **Mixing up input depth $D_{in}$ and output depth $D_{out}$** in the parameter formula. $D_{in}$ is the depth of the *incoming* volume; $D_{out}$ is the number of *filters* in the current layer.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Softmax/activation function** in architecture diagrams. Always mention the non-linearity between a CONV layer and the next operation — ReLU is the default for KTU answers.
>
> 6. **Writing "feature map" and "activation map" as the same without context.** Strictly, a *feature map* is the linear output of convolution; an *activation map* is what you get *after* applying the non-linearity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Preparation:**

- **CNN = Specialized Deep NN for grid-like data**, using convolution + pooling + FC layers.
- **Three core components:** CONV layer (feature extraction), POOL layer (downsampling), FC layer (classification).
- **Biological inspiration:** Hubel & Wiesel's simple and complex cells in the cat visual cortex.
- **Local Receptive Field:** Each neuron sees only a small patch of the previous layer.
- **Shared Weights:** Same filter applied across the entire input volume — the source of parameter efficiency.
- **Feature Map:** The 2D output of one filter; one feature map per filter.
- **Receptive Field Growth:** Deeper neurons see a larger portion of the original image.
- **Key Dimension Formula:** $W_{out} = \lfloor (W_{in} - F + 2P) / S \rfloor + 1$ — memorize and apply correctly.
- **Key Parameter Formula:** $P_{conv} = (F \cdot F \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot D_{out}$ — don't forget the **+1 bias**.
- **ReLU** is the standard non-linearity between CONV and POOL.
- **Softmax** is the standard output activation for multi-class classification.
- **Pooling is separate from convolution** — it is a fixed (non-learnable) downsampling operation.
- **Hierarchy of features:** Low-level (edges) → Mid-level (textures, parts) → High-level (whole objects).
- **Advantages over ANN:** Parameter efficiency, translation equivariance, automatic feature learning, spatial structure preservation.
- **Real-world applications:** Medical imaging, autonomous driving, face recognition, satellite imagery, industrial defect detection, OCR.

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) - Basic Concept and Simple Architecture with Practical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) - Basic Concept and Simple Architecture with Practical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a specialized class of artificial neural networks designed to process **sequential data** (time series, text, speech, video frames) by maintaining an internal **hidden state** that acts as a form of dynamic memory. Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs exhibit **temporal dynamic behaviour** because the same set of weights is applied recursively across every element of a sequence, and connections between units form a directed cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Recurrent Neural Networks form the foundation of sequence modelling. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to (i) describe the basic RNN cell, (ii) understand the unrolled computational graph, (iii) write the forward-pass equations, and (iv) explain vanishing/exploding gradient issues at a conceptual level.

Mathematically, an RNN processes an input sequence $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(T)}$ and at each time step $t$ maintains a hidden state vector $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ computed as:

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = f_H\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)
$$

where $f_H(\cdot)$ is a nonlinear activation (typically **tanh** or **ReLU**), $\mathbf{W}_{hx}$ is the input-to-hidden weight matrix, $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is the hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight matrix, and $\mathbf{b}_h$ is the bias vector.

The corresponding output at time $t$ is:

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = f_O\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y\right)
$$

where $f_O(\cdot)$ is the output activation (e.g., **softmax** for classification).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Story Word by Word"

Imagine you are reading a sentence: *"The clouds were dark, so I took my ___"*. To predict the blank, you do **not** read each word in isolation — you remember the words you have already seen. The words *dark* and *clouds* shift your expectation from "sunhat" toward "umbrella."

An RNN behaves identically:
- **Input ($\mathbf{x}^{(t)}$):** The current word (or current time-step value).
- **Hidden state ($\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$):** Your evolving mental summary of everything read so far.
- **Output ($\mathbf{y}^{(t)}$):** Your prediction (or classification) at that instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Sequential decisions appear everywhere — predictive maintenance on a turbine's vibration signal, ECG arrhythmia detection, stock-price forecasting, autonomous-vehicle trajectory prediction, and natural-language command interpretation in smart devices.

## 1.3 Core Components in an RNN Cell

| Component | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Input vector at step $t$ | $\mathbf{x}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Carries current observation |
| Hidden state at step $t$ | $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ | Acts as **memory** |
| Output vector at step $t$ | $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ | Task-specific prediction |
| Input weight matrix | $\mathbf{W}_{hx} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$ | Maps $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow \mathbf{h}$ |
| Recurrent weight matrix | $\mathbf{W}_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ | Maps $\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} \rightarrow \mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ |
| Output weight matrix | $\mathbf{W}_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ | Maps $\mathbf{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{y}$ |
| Hidden bias | $\mathbf{b}_h \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ | Shifts the hidden pre-activation |
| Output bias | $\mathbf{b}_y \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ | Shifts the output pre-activation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Sharing — the Defining Property of RNNs**
> The three matrices $\mathbf{W}_{hx}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ (and biases) are the **same** at every time step $t$. This is called **weight sharing across time**, and it is what allows an RNN trained on short sequences to generalize to longer ones at inference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Equivalent Views of an RNN

RNNs can be visualised in two mathematically equivalent ways. Examiners regularly test this distinction.

### (A) Folded (Recurrent) View

A single cell with a self-loop representing the recurrence.

### (B) Unrolled (Computational Graph) View

The same cell is replicated $T$ times — once for each element of the sequence — with the hidden state passed forward as an arrow from step $t{-}1$ to step $t$.

## 2.2 Logical Operational Steps (Forward Pass)

1. **Initialise** the hidden state $\mathbf{h}^{(0)}$ — usually as a zero vector.
2. **For each time step** $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:
    - Compute the pre-activation: $\mathbf{a}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h$.
    - Apply nonlinearity: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = f_H(\mathbf{a}^{(t)})$.
    - Compute the output: $\mathbf{o}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y$.
    - Apply output activation: $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = f_O(\mathbf{o}^{(t)})$.
3. **Compute the loss** $\mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ between prediction $\mathbf{y}^{(t)}$ and ground truth $\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)}$.
4. **Aggregate** the loss over the sequence: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.

## 2.3 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

Training is performed by **unrolling the network** through all $T$ time steps and applying the standard backpropagation chain rule across the time axis — hence the name **Backpropagation Through Time**.

The gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the recurrent weight $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ involves a **sum of products of Jacobians** across all earlier time steps:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k \le t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(t)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}} \cdot \left(\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

The product term $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}$ is the root cause of the **vanishing / exploding gradient problem**.

## 2.4 Why Vanilla RNNs Struggle — Vanishing & Exploding Gradients

If the largest singular value of the recurrent Jacobian $\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}$ is denoted by $\rho$, then for a gap of $\tau$ time steps:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=t-\tau+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \;\approx\; \rho^{\tau}
$$

- If $\rho > 1$ → gradient grows as $\rho^{\tau}$ → **exploding gradients**.
- If $\rho < 1$ → gradient shrinks as $\rho^{\tau}$ → **vanishing gradients** (the dominant failure mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering remedy in practice:** *gradient clipping* (for explosion) and gating mechanisms such as **LSTM** (Long Short-Term Memory) and **GRU** (Gated Recurrent Unit) for vanishing gradients.

## 2.5 Loss Functions Used with RNNs

| Task Type | Per-step Loss $\mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Output Activation $f_O$ |
|---|---|---|
| Binary classification | $-\left[\hat{y}^{(t)} \log y^{(t)} + (1-\hat{y}^{(t)})\log(1-y^{(t)})\right]$ | sigmoid |
| Multi-class classification | $-\sum_{c} \hat{y}_c^{(t)} \log y_c^{(t)}$ | softmax |
| Regression (continuous) | $\tfrac{1}{2}\left(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}\right)^{2}$ | identity |

Total sequence loss: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = f_H\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$ | Hidden-state update |
| 2 | $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = f_O\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y\right)$ | Output computation |
| 3 | $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ | Standard initialisation |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Total sequence loss |
| 5 | $\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \approx \rho^{(t-k)}$ | Gradient blow-up / decay magnitude |
| 6 | $\rho < 1 \Rightarrow$ vanishing ; $\rho > 1 \Rightarrow$ exploding | Stability condition |
| 7 | $\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)} \approx \arg\max_c \; y_c^{(t)}$ | Final predicted class (softmax) |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Time-series forecasting** — load demand prediction, weather forecasting.
- **Natural Language Processing** — machine translation, sentiment analysis, speech recognition.
- **Bioinformatics** — protein secondary-structure prediction, DNA-sequence modelling.
- **Industrial IoT** — predictive maintenance, anomaly detection in sensor streams.
- **Finance** — algorithmic trading, credit-risk scoring on transaction sequences.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass Through an Unrolled RNN

We unroll the recurrence over $T = 3$ time steps for clarity. Let $\mathbf{x}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$. Activation functions $f_H = \tanh$ and $f_O = \text{softmax}$.

### Step 1 — Time step $t = 1$

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

Since $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_y,\qquad \mathbf{y}^{(1)} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{o}^{(1)})
$$

### Step 2 — Time step $t = 2$

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

Here $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ carries forward the memory from step 1. Substitute its expression:

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right) + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \tanh(\mathbf{a}^{(2)}),\qquad \mathbf{y}^{(2)} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}_y)
$$

### Step 3 — Time step $t = 3$

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(3)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(3)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(3)} = \tanh(\mathbf{a}^{(3)}),\qquad \mathbf{y}^{(3)} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(3)} + \mathbf{b}_y)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each step's pre-activation depends on the *current* input and the *previous* hidden state — propagating memory forward in time.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BPTT Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (Compact Form)

Consider the loss at the **final** time step $T$ only (the extension to summed losses is a sum of identical terms).

We want $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$. By the chain rule, this gradient flows through every intermediate hidden state:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(T)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(T)}} \cdot \left(\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

**Expand the product** by substituting $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} = \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^{2}\right)$ for $f_H = \tanh$:

$$
\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \;=\; \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^{2}\right)
$$

Factorising the constant $\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$:

$$
\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \;=\; \left(\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\right)^{(T-k)} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^{2}\right)
$$

Taking the spectral norm (largest singular value $\rho$):

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \;\le\; \rho^{(T-k)} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{T}\left\Vert\text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^{2}\right)\right\Vert
$$

Since each diagonal entry of $\text{diag}(1 - h_i^{2})$ lies in $[0, 1]$ for tanh, the second product is bounded by $1$. Hence:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \;\le\; \rho^{(T-k)}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The gradient magnitude across a time gap of $(T-k)$ scales as $\rho^{(T-k)}$. This single inequality is the mathematical proof of vanishing ($\rho < 1$) and exploding ($\rho > 1$) gradients — a **favourite 14-mark derivation in KTU exams**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Tiny RNN, $T = 2$

Let:
- $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = [1, 0]^{\top}$, $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = [0, 1]^{\top}$
- $\mathbf{W}_{hx} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hh} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.3\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hy} = [1,\; 1]$
- $\mathbf{b}_h = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{b}_y = 0$, $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = [0, 0]^{\top}$
- $f_H = \tanh$, $f_O = \text{identity}$

### Step 1 — Forward pass at $t = 1$

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}0.4621 \\ 0.1974\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
y^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = (1)(0.4621) + (1)(0.1974) = 0.6595
$$

### Step 2 — Forward pass at $t = 2$

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(1)}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.4\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.3 \cdot 0.4621 \\ 0.3 \cdot 0.1974\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2386 \\ 0.4592\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \tanh\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}0.2386 \\ 0.4592\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}0.2346 \\ 0.4242\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
y^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = 0.2346 + 0.4242 = 0.6588
$$

### Step 3 — Squared-error loss (assume targets $\hat{y}^{(1)} = 1$, $\hat{y}^{(2)} = 1$)

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(1)} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 0.6595)^{2} = 0.0579
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(2)} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 0.6588)^{2} = 0.0582
$$

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}^{(1)} + \mathcal{L}^{(2)} = 0.1161
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Both predictions are pulled toward the same value because the recurrent weight $0.3$ smoothly blends prior memory with current input — exactly the dynamic-memory behaviour we expect from an RNN.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Equations to Engineering Scenarios

| Equation Component | Maps to Engineering Reality |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}$ | Influence of past sensor readings on current decision |
| $\mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)}$ | Influence of the freshly arrived measurement |
| $\tanh(\cdot)$ | Non-linear compression that bounds hidden activations in $[-1, 1]$ |
| $\rho^{(T-k)}$ | How far back in time the network can "remember" during training |
| $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{o}^{(t)})$ | Probability distribution over the next word / next class |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Folded vs Unrolled RNN — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph folded["FOLDED VIEW (single cell with self-loop)"]
        xt_in["x(t)"] --> cellA["RNN Cell"]
        cellA --> yt_out["y(t)"]
        cellA --> hA["h(t)"]
        hA -.recurrent self-loop.-> cellA
    end

    subgraph unrolled["UNROLLED VIEW (computational graph over time)"]
        x1["x(1)"] --> cell1["RNN Cell"]
        cell1 --> h1["h(1)"]
        cell1 --> y1["y(1)"]
        h1 --> cell2["RNN Cell"]
        x2["x(2)"] --> cell2
        cell2 --> h2["h(2)"]
        cell2 --> y2["y(2)"]
        h2 --> cell3["RNN Cell"]
        x3["x(3)"] --> cell3
        cell3 --> h3["h(3)"]
        cell3 --> y3["y(3)"]
    end

    folded -.equivalent representation.-> unrolled
```

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of a Single RNN Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt["x(t)  (input vector)"] --> multA["W_hx . x(t)"]
    htm1["h(t-1)  (previous hidden state)"] --> multB["W_hh . h(t-1)"]
    bias["b_h  (hidden bias)"] --> sumA((+))
    multA --> sumA
    multB --> sumA
    sumA --> act["f_H  =  tanh"]
    act --> ht["h(t)  (new hidden state)"]
    ht --> outMult["W_hy . h(t)"]
    outMult --> outAct["f_O  =  softmax / identity"]
    outAct --> yt["y(t)  (output)"]
    ht -.feeds next time step.-> htm1
```

## 4.3 Sequence-to-Sequence Prediction Topology (Many-to-Many)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["x(1)"] --> rnn1["RNN(1)"]
    rnn1 --> out1["y(1)"]
    rnn1 --> hpass1["h(1)"]
    hpass1 --> rnn2["RNN(2)"]
    in2["x(2)"] --> rnn2
    rnn2 --> out2["y(2)"]
    rnn2 --> hpass2["h(2)"]
    hpass2 --> rnn3["RNN(3)"]
    in3["x(3)"] --> rnn3
    rnn3 --> out3["y(3)"]
    rnn3 --> hpass3["h(3)"]
```

## 4.4 BPTT Information Flow (Gradient Path)
