```mermaid
flowchart RL
    lossL["L(T) at final step"] --> gradOut["dL/dy(T)"]
    gradOut --> gradWhy["dL/dW_hy"]
    gradOut --> gradH["dL/dh(T)"]
    gradH --> jac1["Jacobian W_hh^T . diag(1-h^2)"]
    jac1 --> gradHprev["dL/dh(T-1)"]
    gradHprev --> jac2["Jacobian"]
    jac2 --> gradHprev2["dL/dh(T-2)"]
    gradHprev2 --> gradWhh["dL/dW_hh  (sum over t)"]
    gradWhy --> update["Gradient Update"]
    gradWhh --> update
```

> **Reading aid:** Each `Jacobian` block in the BPTT diagram represents a multiplicative factor along the time axis. Stacking many of them produces the $\rho^{(T-k)}$ decay/blow-up behaviour derived in Section 3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between a feedforward neural network and a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). Mention any two applications of RNN.**

**Model Answer (concise, exam-ready):**

| Aspect | Feedforward NN | RNN |
|---|---|---|
| Connection pattern | Acyclic — no feedback edges | Has feedback (recurrent) edges |
| Memory | Stateless | Stateful — hidden state $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ |
| Input type | Fixed-size vector | Variable-length sequence |
| Parameter sharing across time | No | Yes — same $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ at every step |

**Applications:** (i) Machine translation, (ii) Speech recognition, (iii) Time-series forecasting (any two).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the vanishing-gradient problem in RNNs? How does it affect long-sequence learning?**

**Model Answer:**

During **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, the gradient flowing back through $T{-}k$ time steps is multiplied by the recurrent Jacobian $\approx T - k$ times. The magnitude therefore scales as $\rho^{(T-k)}$ where $\rho < 1$ is the dominant singular value. As $T - k$ grows:

$$
\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}} \right\Vert \;\propto\; \rho^{(T-k)} \;\xrightarrow[T-k \to \infty]{}\; 0
$$

Effect: weights responsible for **long-range dependencies** receive vanishingly small updates, so the network effectively forgets the distant past. Practical remedies include LSTM, GRU, orthogonal initialisation of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$, and gradient clipping.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts — typically **7 + 7** marks. Internal choice between Q-A and Q-B is offered on the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** *Explain the basic architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network with a neatly labelled folded and unrolled diagram. Write the forward-pass equations. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive the gradient of the loss with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ using BPTT and explain how it leads to the vanishing-gradient problem. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

A Recurrent Neural Network processes a sequence by maintaining a hidden state that is updated at every time step. The **folded view** shows a single cell with a self-loop, while the **unrolled view** replicates the cell across all $T$ steps of the input sequence, with the hidden state passed from one replica to the next.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1["Folded"]
        xt["x(t)"] --> cA["Cell"]
        cA --> yt["y(t)"]
        cA --> ht["h(t)"]
        ht -.loop.-> cA
    end
    subgraph A2["Unrolled"]
        x1["x(1)"] --> c1["Cell"]
        c1 --> h1["h(1)"] --> c2["Cell"]
        x2["x(2)"] --> c2
        c2 --> h2["h(2)"] --> c3["Cell"]
        x3["x(3)"] --> c3
        c3 --> h3["h(3)"]
    end
```

**Forward-pass equations** (with $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$):

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y\right)
$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

By the chain rule applied across time:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(T)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(T)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(T)}} \cdot \left(\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

Substituting $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} = \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^{2}\right)$ and taking the norm:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \;\le\; \rho^{(T-k)}
$$

When $\rho < 1$, the gradient decays exponentially as the time gap $T-k$ increases → **vanishing gradients** → the network cannot learn long-range dependencies. When $\rho > 1$, the gradient grows exponentially → **exploding gradients**, mitigated by gradient clipping.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

**(a)** *Consider a vanilla RNN with $\mathbf{W}_{hx} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.1\\0.2 & 0.4\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hh} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3 & 0\\0 & 0.3\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hy} = [1\;\;1]$, biases zero, $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = [0,0]^{\top}$, and inputs $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = [1,0]^{\top}$, $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = [0,1]^{\top}$. With $f_H = \tanh$ and $f_O = \text{identity}$, compute $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}, \mathbf{h}^{(2)}, y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}$. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A company wants to predict the next day's electricity load using the previous 30 days' hourly readings. Briefly justify why an RNN is suitable, list the architectural choices you would make (input/output sizes, activation, loss), and explain how you would mitigate the vanishing-gradient issue. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**At $t = 1$:**

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \tanh\!\begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.4621 \\ 0.1974\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
y^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = 0.4621 + 0.1974 = 0.6595
$$

**At $t = 2$:**

$$
\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.4\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.3 \cdot 0.4621 \\ 0.3 \cdot 0.1974\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2386 \\ 0.4592\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \tanh\!\begin{bmatrix}0.2386 \\ 0.4592\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2346 \\ 0.4242\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
y^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = 0.2346 + 0.4242 = 0.6588
$$

**Final answers:**

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.4621 \\ 0.1974\end{bmatrix},\quad
\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2346 \\ 0.4242\end{bmatrix},\quad
y^{(1)} = 0.6595,\quad
y^{(2)} = 0.6588
$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Why RNN is suitable:** The load at time $t$ depends not only on the most recent reading but also on daily/weekly patterns stretching across the entire 30-day window. An RNN's recurrent hidden state $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ can capture such temporal dependencies, and its **parameter sharing across time** lets it generalise across the 24-hour daily cycle.

**Architectural choices:**

| Element | Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Input size | 1 (hourly load in MW) | Single-feature univariate series |
| Sequence length | $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ | One month of hourly readings |
| Hidden size | 32 – 128 (tunable) | Trade-off between capacity & overfitting |
| Output size | 1 (next-hour load) | One-step-ahead forecast |
| $f_H$ | tanh or ReLU | tanh preferred for stable hidden states |
| $f_O$ | identity (regression) | Continuous load value |
| Loss | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | Standard regression objective |

**Mitigation of vanishing gradients:**
1. Use **LSTM** or **GRU** cells — gating controls information flow and keeps gradients bounded.
2. **Gradient clipping** by global norm to prevent explosion.
3. Initialise $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ as an **orthogonal matrix** so that $\rho \approx 1$.
4. Use **layer-normalisation** or **batch-normalisation** across time.
5. Optionally use **teacher forcing** during early training epochs.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU examiners deduct marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "recurrent" with "recursive"** — RNNs process a sequence in time order; recursive NNs apply the same function on a tree structure.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$** — without this initial condition, the unrolled equations are incomplete. (1 mark lost routinely.)
> 3. **Drawing only one of the two views** — examiners expect both folded and unrolled diagrams; missing one costs up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing BPTT without the product term** — the term $\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}$ is *the* key part of the derivation. Skipping it is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up which weights are shared across time** — only $\mathbf{W}_{hx}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}, \mathbf{b}_h, \mathbf{b}_y$ are shared; the hidden states themselves differ at every step.
> 6. **Stating "vanishing gradients" without explaining *why* ($\rho < 1$)** — the spectral-radius inequality is what examiners want to see in the derivation.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **RNN** is a neural network for **sequential data**, distinguished by its **recurrent hidden state** $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$.
- ✅ The defining property is **parameter sharing across time** — the same $\mathbf{W}_{hx}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ are reused at every step.
- ✅ Two equivalent views: **folded** (compact) and **unrolled** (computational graph over $T$ steps).
- ✅ Forward pass: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = f_H(\mathbf{W}_{hx}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h)$, $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = f_O(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y)$.
- ✅ Initialisation: $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{0}$ in the standard formulation.
- ✅ Training algorithm: **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** — gradient flows backward through every unrolled time step.
- ✅ BPTT gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ contains a product of Jacobians of length $(T - k)$ — the source of instability.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradients** dominate when $\rho < 1$; **exploding gradients** when $\rho > 1$.
- ✅ Remedies: gradient clipping, orthogonal initialisation, LSTM/GRU cells, layer-normalisation.
- ✅ Sequence loss is the sum of per-step losses: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.
- ✅ Vanilla RNNs struggle with long sequences (>50–100 steps) — LSTM/GRU are the industry-standard upgrades (covered in Module 5).
- ✅ Applications to remember for KTU viva: machine translation, speech recognition, ECG anomaly detection, load forecasting, stock prediction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LSTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LSTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized type of **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** architecture introduced by *Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)* and later refined by *Gers et al. (2000)*. It is engineered to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in conventional RNNs by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by a sophisticated system of **gates**.

The fundamental unit of an LSTM is the **memory cell (or cell state)** — denoted $\mathbf{C}_t$ — which acts as a "conveyor belt" that carries relevant information across long sequences. The flow of information into, out of, and through this cell is controlled by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms**:

1. **Forget Gate** ($\mathbf{f}_t$) — decides what to *erase* from the cell state.
2. **Input Gate** ($\mathbf{i}_t$) — decides what *new information* to write into the cell state.
3. **Output Gate** ($\mathbf{o}_t$) — decides what to *expose* as the hidden output $\mathbf{h}_t$ at the current time step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Sequence Models):** LSTM is a **mandatory** sub-topic. Expect direct 3-mark definitions and 7-14 mark derivations/architecture questions. The gating equations and cell-state update rule form the **highest-weightage** portion of any LSTM question.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Smart Secretary

Imagine you are a CEO reading 1000 pages of a market report, page by page. A **standard RNN** is like a secretary with **photographic but leaky memory** — by page 100, she has forgotten the financial figures from page 1. A **human** has selective memory: she remembers the *revenue numbers* but forgets the *font size details*.

An **LSTM cell** is exactly that **smart secretary**:

- 🗑️ **Forget Gate** → *"Throw out the irrelevant trivia from earlier pages."*
- ✍️ **Input Gate** → *"Carefully note down the new important facts from the current page."*
- 📤 **Output Gate** → *"Based on what I remember, here is the precise answer to your current question."*

The **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ is her **notebook** that persists across all pages (time steps), while the **hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_t$ is the **answer she gives** at each step.

## 1.3 Why Was LSTM Needed? — The Vanishing Gradient Problem

In a vanilla RNN, the hidden state update is:
$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) multiplies the recurrent weight matrix's derivatives across $T$ steps. For an **L2-norm spectral radius** of the Jacobian, $\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{t-1}} \right)$:

- If $\rho < 1$ → gradients **shrink exponentially** → *vanishing gradient* (long-term dependencies lost).
- If $\rho > 1$ → gradients **blow up** → *exploding gradient* (training instability).

LSTM solves this by replacing the multiplicative state-update with an **additive update**, which yields a constant error carousel (CEC) — gradients flow across time steps without exponential decay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** The **sigmoid** $\sigma$ squashes values to $[0,1]$, where **0 = "block completely"** and **1 = "let everything through"**. The **tanh** squashes to $[-1,1]$ — it is used to *write candidate values* into the cell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LSTM Cell — Operational Breakdown

An LSTM cell at time step $t$ receives **three inputs** and produces **two outputs**:

- **Inputs**: previous hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$, previous cell state $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$, and current input $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
- **Outputs**: current hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ and current cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$.

### Step 1 — Forget Gate
Acts as a **sigmoid mask** over the old cell state. Multiplies element-wise with $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$.

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)$$

- $\mathbf{f}_t \in [0,1]^{n}$ where $n$ is the cell-state dimension.
- A value of **0** means *"completely forget"*, **1** means *"perfectly retain"*.

### Step 2 — Input Gate
Decides **which candidate values** will be written. Two parts: a sigmoid filter and a tanh candidate.

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)$$
$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_C \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C\right)$$

- $\mathbf{i}_t$ is a **filter**, $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ is the **new information** to be added.

### Step 3 — Cell State Update (Core LSTM Equation)
The new cell state is the **additive combination** of retained old memory and new candidates:

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

- $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.
- The **+** here is the key innovation — it prevents gradient decay.

### Step 4 — Output Gate
Decides what part of the cell state becomes the next hidden state.

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\right)$$
$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

| Design Choice | Reason |
|---------------|--------|
| Sigmoid for gates $\mathbf{f}, \mathbf{i}, \mathbf{o}$ | Outputs in $[0,1]$ — perfect for multiplicative masking. |
| Tanh for $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ and $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Outputs in $[-1,1]$ — zero-centered, helps gradient flow. |
| Additive cell update ($\mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$) | Creates a **linear path** for gradient flow — solves vanishing gradient. |
| Three gates (not one) | Allows **separate control** of memory *retention*, *writing*, and *reading*. |
| Bias initialization $\mathbf{b}_f = \mathbf{1}$ | Encourages the network to **retain memory by default** at the start of training. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Key:** All weight matrices $\mathbf{W}$ have shape $(\text{hidden\_size}, \text{hidden\_size} + \text{input\_size})$. The bracketed term $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t]$ is the **concatenation** of the two vectors.

| # | Component | Equation | Output Range | Purpose |
|---|-----------|----------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | Forget Gate | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | $[0,1]^{n}$ | Erase old memory |
| 2 | Input Gate | $\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$ | $[0,1]^{n}$ | Filter new info |
| 3 | Candidate Memory | $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C)$ | $[-1,1]^{n}$ | New values to write |
| 4 | Cell State Update | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ | New long-term memory |
| 5 | Output Gate | $\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$ | $[0,1]^{n}$ | Read filter |
| 6 | Hidden State | $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ | $[-1,1]^{n}$ | Output / next-step input |
| 7 | Total Parameters per Cell | $4 \times (n^2 + n \cdot d + n)$ | — | Trainable weights |

Where $n$ = hidden size, $d$ = input size.

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Applications

| Domain | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **Natural Language Processing** | Machine translation, sentiment analysis, named-entity recognition, text generation. |
| **Speech Recognition** | Acoustic modeling in systems like Google's Voice Search. |
| **Time-Series Forecasting** | Stock price prediction, energy load forecasting, weather prediction. |
| **Medical Diagnostics** | ECG/EEG signal classification, patient monitoring. |
| **Music Generation** | Compositional models (e.g., Google's Magenta). |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Industrial IoT sensor stream analysis. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Variants for KTU Awareness:**
> - **Peephole LSTM** (Gers & Schmidhuber, 2002): Gates see the cell state $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$ directly.
> - **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** (Cho et al., 2014): Combines forget and input gates into a single **update gate**; merges cell and hidden state — fewer parameters, often comparable performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Additive Cell Update Solves Vanishing Gradients

We want to analyze the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ at time step $t$ with respect to the cell state at an earlier time step $k < t$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_k}$$

### Step 1: Chain Rule Expansion Across Time
By applying the chain rule repeatedly across the intermediate cell states, we get:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_j}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{j-1}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Cell State Update
From equation (4), $\mathbf{C}_j = \mathbf{f}_j \odot \mathbf{C}_{j-1} + \mathbf{i}_j \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_j$.

Therefore, the partial derivative with respect to $\mathbf{C}_{j-1}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_j}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(\mathbf{f}_j\right) + \text{other terms involving } \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_j \text{ and } \mathbf{f}_j \text{'s derivatives}$$

### Step 3: Key Insight — The Forget-Gate Path
The **dominant term** in the gradient product is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_k} \approx \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(\mathbf{f}_j\right)$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Product
Since each $\mathbf{f}_j \in [0,1]^{n}$ element-wise:

- The product $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{f}_j$ decays **multiplicatively** but is **bounded** because each element is at most 1.
- Critically, the **network can learn to set $\mathbf{f}_j \approx 1$** for dimensions that need long-range memory, preserving the gradient.
- Compare this to vanilla RNN, where the product is over $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (unconstrained spectral radius) — gradients vanish/explode uncontrollably.

### Step 5: Conclusion
**The cell state update is an *additive* (not multiplicative) operation on $\mathbf{C}_{j-1}$ through the forget-gate path.** This additive structure, combined with learned gate activations, gives LSTM its hallmark ability to model **long-range dependencies** spanning hundreds of time steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward-Pass Matrix Form (Concatenation Convention)

Let $\mathbf{z}_t = [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+d)}$ be the column-stacked concatenation. Define stacked weights $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{W}_f; \mathbf{W}_i; \mathbf{W}_C; \mathbf{W}_o] \in \mathbb{R}^{(4n) \times (n+d)}$.

Then the four pre-activations can be compactly written as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{a}_t &= \mathbf{W} \cdot \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{W}_f \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b}_f \\ \mathbf{W}_i \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b}_i \\ \mathbf{W}_C \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b}_C \\ \mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b}_o \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a}_t^{(f)} \\ \mathbf{a}_t^{(i)} \\ \mathbf{a}_t^{(C)} \\ \mathbf{a}_t^{(o)} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply element-wise nonlinearities **slice-wise**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{a}_t^{(f)}) \\
\mathbf{i}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{a}_t^{(i)}) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t &= \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t^{(C)}) \\
\mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma(\mathbf{a}_t^{(o)})
\end{aligned}$$

This consolidation is the **standard efficient-implementation form** used in deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) Gradients

For a sequence loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$, the gradients w.r.t. each gate's pre-activation are:

| Gradient | Formula | Meaning |
|----------|---------|---------|
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{a}_t^{(o)}}$ | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{a}_t^{(o)})$ | Update output gate |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{a}_t^{(C)}}$ | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \odot \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tanh'(\mathbf{a}_t^{(C)})$ | Update candidate |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{a}_t^{(i)}}$ | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{a}_t^{(i)})$ | Update input gate |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{a}_t^{(f)}}$ | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{a}_t^{(f)})$ | Update forget gate |

where $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ and $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$.

The **recursive gradient** of the loss w.r.t. the cell state is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \odot \mathbf{f}_t + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \odot \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh'(\mathbf{C}_t) \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{t-1}}\bigg|_{\text{via } \mathbf{h}_{t-1}}$$

The **additive** $\mathbf{f}_t \odot$ term is what makes the long-term gradient signal **stable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LSTM Cell — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt[/xt - Current Input/] --> concat1[Concat with h_t-1]
    htm1[/h_t-1 - Prev Hidden/] --> concat1
    concat1 --> Wf[Wf]
    concat1 --> Wi[Wi]
    concat1 --> WC[WC]
    concat1 --> Wo[Wo]
    Wf --> SigmoidF[Sigma - Forget Gate]
    Wi --> SigmoidI[Sigma - Input Gate]
    WC --> TanhC[Tanh - Candidate]
    Wo --> SigmoidO[Sigma - Output Gate]
    Ctm1[/C_t-1 - Prev Cell State/] --> Multiply1
    SigmoidF --> Multiply1((Hadamard Product))
    Multiply1 --> AddSum((Sum))
    SigmoidI --> Multiply2((Hadamard Product))
    TanhC --> Multiply2
    Multiply2 --> AddSum
    AddSum --> Ct[/C_t - New Cell State/]
    Ct --> TanhOut[Tanh]
    SigmoidO --> Multiply3((Hadamard Product))
    TanhOut --> Multiply3
    Multiply3 --> ht[/h_t - New Hidden State/]
    ht --> OutNext[To Next Time Step]
    Ct --> OutNextC[To Next Time Step]
```

## 4.2 Unrolled LSTM Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t1 [Time Step t-1]
        xtm1[/x_t-1/] --> celltm1[LSTM Cell]
        ctm1[/C_t-1/] --> celltm1
        celltm1 --> htm1[/h_t-1/]
    end
    subgraph t2 [Time Step t]
        xt[/x_t/] --> cellt[LSTM Cell]
        htm1 --> cellt
        ctm1 --> cellt
        cellt --> ht[/h_t/]
        cellt --> ct[/C_t/]
    end
    subgraph t3 [Time Step t+1]
        xtp1[/x_t+1/] --> celltp1[LSTM Cell]
        ht --> celltp1
        ct --> celltp1
        celltp1 --> htp1[/h_t+1/]
    end
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Summary

| Stage | Mathematical Operation | Geometric Intuition |
|-------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Concat | $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t]$ | Stack past and present into a single vector. |
| Gate pre-act | $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{z}_t + \mathbf{b}$ | Project into gate space. |
| Non-linearity | $\sigma$ / $\tanh$ | Squash to a controlled range. |
| Combine | $\mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | Additive memory write. |
| Output | $\mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ | Filtered read-out. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two $\tanh$?** The internal candidate $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ uses $\tanh$ to *write* values in $[-1,1]$. The output layer also uses $\tanh$ on the cell state to *compress* it before the output gate filters it. The double use ensures symmetric, zero-centered activations throughout.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Why is it preferred over a conventional RNN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LSTM is a type of recurrent neural network architecture that uses **gated memory cells** to retain long-term dependencies in sequential data. It overcomes the **vanishing gradient problem** of conventional RNNs by using:
- A **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ that carries information across time steps with **additive updates**.
- Three **gating mechanisms** (forget, input, output) that regulate the flow of information.

Unlike conventional RNNs, where gradients decay exponentially over long sequences, the **additive structure** in LSTM's cell state allows gradients to flow stably across many time steps. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three gates of an LSTM cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Forget Gate ($\mathbf{f}_t$):** Decides what information to **discard** from the cell state. Computed as $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$. A value of 0 means "forget completely."
2. **Input Gate ($\mathbf{i}_t$):** Decides what **new information** to store. Computed as $\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$.
3. **Output Gate ($\mathbf{o}_t$):** Decides what to **output** as the hidden state. Computed as $\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$, and the hidden state is $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$. *(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **LSTM cell**. Clearly label the forget gate, input gate, output gate, cell state, and hidden state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward-pass equations** for all four components (forget gate, input gate, candidate cell state, and cell state update) of an LSTM cell. Show how the **output gate** produces the final hidden state. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **vanishing gradient problem** in conventional RNNs. Show mathematically how the **additive cell-state update** in LSTM mitigates this issue during **backpropagation through time (BPTT)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **LSTM** with **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**. Tabulate the architectural differences and list **three real-world applications** of LSTM in sequence modeling. **(7 marks)**

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Solution A(a): LSTM Cell Architecture (7 marks)

The LSTM cell consists of:
- **Inputs**: $\mathbf{x}_t$ (current input), $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ (previous hidden state), $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$ (previous cell state).
- **Three gates**: Forget, Input, Output — each implemented as a sigmoid layer taking concatenated input.
- **Cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$: Long-term memory "conveyor belt."
- **Hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_t$: Filtered output exposed to the rest of the network.

**Block diagram (text description for 4 marks):**

```
       x_t ──┐
              ├──[concat]──┬──σ──> f_t ──┐
       h_t-1 ─┘            ├──σ──> i_t ──┐    ┌─────────┐
                          ├──tanh-> C̃_t ─┼───│ ⊙ + ⊙ │──> C_t ─┬──> tanh ──┐
              (Cell)──────┴──σ──> o_t ──┘    └─────────┘         └──> ⊙ ──> h_t
              C_t-1 ──> [f_t ⊙ C_t-1]                                      
```

**Labels and explanations (3 marks):**
- *Forget gate* $\mathbf{f}_t$ multiplies element-wise with $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$ to erase information.
- *Input gate* $\mathbf{i}_t$ and *candidate* $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ are combined to write new info.
- *Output gate* $\mathbf{o}_t$ controls what part of $\tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ becomes $\mathbf{h}_t$.

---

### Solution A(b): Forward-Pass Derivation (7 marks)

**Forget Gate** (1.5 marks):
$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)$$
where $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t]$ denotes concatenation.

**Input Gate** (1.5 marks):
$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)$$

**Candidate Cell State** (1.5 marks):
$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C\right)$$

**Cell State Update** (1.5 marks):
$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

**Output Gate and Hidden State** (1 mark):
$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\right), \quad \mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$$

---

### Solution B(a): Vanishing Gradient & BPTT Analysis (7 marks)

**Vanishing gradient in vanilla RNN (3 marks):**
In an RNN, the hidden state update is $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b})$. During BPTT, the gradient w.r.t. an earlier hidden state involves:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}}$$

The Jacobian $\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}}$ involves $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ and $\tanh'$. If the spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh} < 1$, the product **decays exponentially** — the network cannot learn long-term dependencies.

**LSTM's additive update solution (4 marks):**
In LSTM, the analogous gradient flows through the cell state. The key cell-state gradient term is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_j}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{j-1}} = \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_j) + (\text{other terms})$$

The dominant path is:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_k} \approx \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_j)$$

Because $\mathbf{f}_j \in [0,1]$ are **learned parameters**, the network can set them close to 1 for dimensions that need to preserve long-term memory. The **additive** nature of $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ ensures that even if some dimensions decay, others can be preserved — solving vanishing gradient.

---

### Solution B(b): LSTM vs GRU & Applications (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|---------|------|-----|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Cell & hidden state | Separate ($\mathbf{C}_t$ and $\mathbf{h}_t$) | Combined ($\mathbf{h}_t$ only) |
| Total parameters | $4(n^2 + nd + n)$ | $3(n^2 + nd + n)$ |
| Output mechanism | $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ | $\mathbf{h}_t = (1-\mathbf{z}_t)\odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ |
| Peephole connections | Optional | Not present |
| Memory control | Fine-grained (write/read/forget) | Coarse (update/reset) |
| Training speed | Slower (more params) | Faster |
| Long-sequence perf. | Generally better | Comparable, slightly less powerful |

**Three real-world applications (2 marks):**
1. **Machine Translation** (e.g., Google Translate's seq2seq models).
2. **Speech Recognition** (acoustic modeling in voice assistants).
3. **Stock Price Forecasting** (time-series prediction in financial analytics).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing $\mathbf{f}_t$ with $\mathbf{i}_t$**: The forget gate uses $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$, the input gate uses $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ — students often swap these and lose **2 marks** in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tanh$ on $\mathbf{C}_t$ before output**: The hidden state equation is $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$, **not** $\mathbf{o}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_t$. This single omission costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not initializing forget-gate bias to 1**: Initializing $\mathbf{b}_f = \mathbf{0}$ is a common bug — KTU examiners may deduct marks for omitting this **engineering best practice**.
> 4. **Mixing up $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t]$ concatenation vs. addition**: Always **concatenate**, then multiply by the weight matrix. Many students write $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}_t$ — this is **wrong** unless weights are explicitly split.
> 5. **Saying "LSTM has no vanishing gradient"**: LSTM *mitigates* it via learned gates; it does not *eliminate* it. This nuance can cost **0.5–1 mark** in long-answer questions.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Must Remember | Quick Recall Trigger |
|---|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | LSTM = RNN with **gated memory cell**. | "Smart secretary" analogy. |
| 2 | **Three gates**: forget, input, output — all use $\sigma$. | "3G of LSTM." |
| 3 | Cell state update: $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$. | Additive = no vanishing gradient. |
| 4 | Hidden state: $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$. | Output gate filters. |
| 5 | Candidate: $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C)$. | Always $\tanh$ for writing. |
| 6 | $\sigma \in [0,1]$, $\tanh \in [-1,1]$. | Don't mix them up. |
| 7 | $\odot$ = **Hadamard product** (element-wise). | Not matrix multiplication. |
| 8 | Total params per cell = $4(n^2 + nd + n)$. | $4\times$ a vanilla-RNN cell. |
| 9 | GRU has **2 gates** and **no separate cell state**. | Lighter alternative. |
| 10 | Initialize $\mathbf{b}_f = \mathbf{1}$ in practice. | Default behavior = retain memory. |

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked *"How does LSTM solve vanishing gradient?"*, **always** mention three things: (1) the **additive** cell update, (2) the **learned forget gate** that can be set close to 1, and (3) the **constant error carousel (CEC)** created by the linear path through $\mathbf{C}_t$. Missing any of these = partial credit loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a simplified gating mechanism in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**, that uses **two gates** — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — to control the flow of information through time, thereby mitigating the **vanishing gradient problem** while reducing the parameter count compared to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks.

Mathematically, a GRU cell maintains a single hidden state vector $h_t$ and employs two learned gating vectors to decide *what to keep*, *what to throw away*, and *how to combine past memory with new input*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Secretary"

Imagine you are a **busy executive's smart secretary** (the GRU cell) reading emails (input $x_t$) one by one, while maintaining a mental notebook of past meetings (hidden state $h_{t-1}$):

- **Update Gate ($z_t$)** acts like a *switchboard operator*. It decides: *"Should I keep my old notebook notes, or overwrite them with the new email's information?"* A value of 1 means "completely replace"; a value of 0 means "ignore the new email entirely."
- **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** acts like an *eraser*. It decides: *"Should I erase some old notes before combining them with the new email to form my understanding?"* It allows the model to forget irrelevant past context when computing the new candidate understanding.
- **Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$)** is the secretary's *draft note* after reading the new email, potentially with some old notes erased.
- **Final Hidden State ($h_t$)** is the *updated official notebook* — a convex interpolation (weighted average) between the old notebook and the draft note.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GRU is covered under Module 4 alongside LSTM as a *gated recurrent architecture*. Students must be able to draw the **GRU cell architecture**, write the **four governing equations**, and explain the role of **each gate** with respect to the vanishing gradient problem.

## 1.3 Why GRU Exists — The Motivation

Standard RNNs suffer from the **vanishing gradient problem**, where backpropagated gradients shrink exponentially through time, making it nearly impossible to learn **long-term dependencies**. LSTM solved this with three gates and a separate cell state. GRU solves the same problem with a **leaner architecture** — only two gates and a single state vector — offering:

- **Fewer parameters** (roughly 25% less than LSTM)
- **Faster training** and inference
- **Comparable performance** on many sequence tasks (speech, music, text)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components

A GRU cell at time step $t$ receives two inputs:
1. The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$
2. The current input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$

It produces one output:
- The current hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$

Internally, three learned transformations occur.

## 2.2 The Four Governing Equations

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **four canonical GRU equations** that KTU examiners expect verbatim in the exam.

**Step 1 — Update Gate:**
$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

- Output range: $(0, 1)$ element-wise due to the **sigmoid activation $\sigma$**.
- Decides *how much* of the past to carry forward.

**Step 2 — Reset Gate:**
$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

- Output range: $(0, 1)$ element-wise.
- Decides *how much* of the past to *forget* when computing the candidate state.

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State:**
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

- Output range: $(-1, 1)$ element-wise due to the **hyperbolic tangent $\tanh$**.
- The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.
- Multiplying $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ lets the reset gate zero-out irrelevant past dimensions.

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State (Linear Interpolation):**
$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

- This is a **convex combination** of the old state and the new candidate.
- When $z_t \to 0$: $h_t \approx h_{t-1}$ (pure memory pass-through).
- When $z_t \to 1$: $h_t \approx \tilde{h}_t$ (pure new candidate).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimensions | Activation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Input vector at time $t$ | $m \times 1$ | None |
| $h_{t-1}$ | Previous hidden state | $n \times 1$ | None |
| $z_t$ | Update gate output | $n \times 1$ | $\sigma$ (sigmoid) |
| $r_t$ | Reset gate output | $n \times 1$ | $\sigma$ (sigmoid) |
| $\tilde{h}_t$ | Candidate hidden state | $n \times 1$ | $\tanh$ |
| $h_t$ | Final hidden state at time $t$ | $n \times 1$ | Linear (identity) |
| $W_z, W_r, W$ | Learnable weight matrices | $n \times (n+m)$ | None |
| $b_z, b_r, b$ | Learnable bias vectors | $n \times 1$ | None |
| $\odot$ | Hadamard (element-wise) product | — | — |
| $[a, b]$ | Concatenation of vectors $a$ and $b$ | $(n+m) \times 1$ | — |

## 2.4 Parameter Count Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"How many trainable parameters does a GRU cell have?"*

Each weight matrix is of size $n \times (n+m)$ and each bias is of size $n \times 1$.

- $W_z$: $n(n+m)$ weights + $n$ biases
- $W_r$: $n(n+m)$ weights + $n$ biases
- $W$: $n(n+m)$ weights + $n$ biases

**Total parameters per GRU cell:**
$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \times n(n+m) + 3n = 3n^2 + 3nm + 3n$$

**Comparison with LSTM:** An LSTM cell has **four** gating weight matrices plus a candidate cell-state matrix, totaling $4n(n+m) + 4n$ parameters. GRU is therefore **25% more parameter-efficient**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Speech Recognition:** Used in acoustic models for transcribing audio.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock prices, weather, energy load prediction.
- **Music Generation:** Generating note sequences in RNN-based composers.
- **Machine Translation:** Encoder–decoder translation models (Cho et al.'s original 2014 paper).
- **Anomaly Detection in IoT:** Detecting sensor anomalies in industrial monitoring.
- **Text Classification:** Sentiment analysis, spam detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Forward-Pass Derivation

Let us derive the complete forward computation of a GRU cell at time step $t$ for a hidden size $n$ and input size $m$.

### Step 1: Concatenation of Inputs

We first concatenate the previous hidden state and the current input into a single augmented vector:
$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+m) \times 1}$$

This concatenation is the standard practice in gated RNNs to perform a single matrix multiplication instead of two separate ones — saving compute and enabling shared bias terms.

### Step 2: Update Gate Computation

We multiply the concatenated vector by the learnable weight matrix $W_z$ and add the bias $b_z$, then apply the sigmoid activation element-wise:

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

The sigmoid function is defined as:

$$\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$$

which squashes any real-valued input $u$ into the open interval $(0, 1)$. This bounded range is essential because $z_t$ will later be used as a *soft switch* in a convex combination.

### Step 3: Reset Gate Computation

In parallel, the reset gate is computed identically in structure but with its own learned weights $W_r$ and bias $b_r$:

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

### Step 4: Modulated Past State

We compute an *element-wise product* (Hadamard product) of the reset gate and the previous hidden state:

$$r_t \odot h_{t-1}$$

This selectively *zeroes out* components of the past hidden state. When $r_t^{(i)} \approx 0$, the $i$-th dimension of the past is forgotten. When $r_t^{(i)} \approx 1$, the $i$-th dimension of the past is fully preserved.

### Step 5: Candidate Hidden State

We concatenate the modulated past state with the current input, project it through weight matrix $W$ with bias $b$, and apply the $\tanh$ activation:

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

The $\tanh$ activation is defined as:

$$\tanh(u) = \frac{e^{u} - e^{-u}}{e^{u} + e^{-u}}$$

which produces values in $(-1, 1)$. This allows the candidate state to express *positive* and *negative* activations — in contrast to gates that only express *probabilities*.

### Step 6: Final Hidden State via Linear Interpolation

The final hidden state is a *convex combination* (element-wise) of the old state and the new candidate, weighted by the update gate:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

This can be rewritten to expose the *skip-connection* nature:

$$h_t = h_{t-1} - z_t \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t = h_{t-1} + z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$$

The form $h_t = h_{t-1} + z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$ is structurally similar to a **residual connection**, which is precisely why gradients can flow through long sequences without vanishing — the additive term $h_{t-1}$ creates a *gradient highway*.

## 3.2 Backpropagation-Through-Time (BPTT) Gradient Analysis

The key reason GRU works for long sequences lies in the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + \text{residual terms involving } z_t, r_t, W$$

When $z_t$ is small, the dominant term is $(1 - z_t) \approx 1$, so the gradient flows *unchanged* through time. This is the **gradient highway** phenomenon.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Small Toy Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners occasionally pose a 3-mark problem: *"Compute GRU outputs for given inputs and weights."* Let us work a fully numerical example.

**Given:**
- Hidden size $n = 1$, input size $m = 1$.
- $h_{t-1} = 0.5$, $x_t = 1.0$.
- $W_z = 0.3$, $b_z = 0.0$.
- $W_r = 0.4$, $b_r = 0.0$.
- $W = 0.5$, $b = 0.0$.
- (Biases are zero here for simplicity; in real models they are learned.)

**Step 1: Concatenate**
$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] = [0.5,\ 1.0]$$

**Step 2: Update Gate**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot h_{t-1} + W_z' \cdot x_t + b_z) = \sigma(0.3 \cdot 0.5 + 0.3 \cdot 1.0 + 0)$$

Treating $W_z$ as a 1×2 vector $[0.3,\ 0.3]$ for clarity:

$$z_t = \sigma(0.15 + 0.30) = \sigma(0.45) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.45}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6376} \approx 0.6106$$

**Step 3: Reset Gate**

$$r_t = \sigma(0.4 \cdot 0.5 + 0.4 \cdot 1.0) = \sigma(0.20 + 0.40) = \sigma(0.60) \approx 0.6457$$

**Step 4: Candidate State**

$$r_t \odot h_{t-1} = 0.6457 \times 0.5 = 0.3229$$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(0.5 \cdot 0.3229 + 0.5 \cdot 1.0) = \tanh(0.1614 + 0.5000) = \tanh(0.6614) \approx 0.5793$$

**Step 5: Final Hidden State**

$$h_t = (1 - 0.6106) \times 0.5 + 0.6106 \times 0.5793$$
$$h_t = 0.3894 \times 0.5 + 0.6106 \times 0.5793$$
$$h_t = 0.1947 + 0.3537 = 0.5484$$

**Result:** $h_t \approx 0.5484$. The hidden state moved slightly upward from 0.5, consistent with the update gate partially adopting the new candidate value of 0.5793.

## 3.4 GRU vs LSTM — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | GRU | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | **2** (update, reset) | **3** (input, forget, output) |
| State vectors | 1 ($h_t$) | 2 ($h_t$ and $c_t$) |
| Parameters per cell | $3n(n+m) + 3n$ | $4n(n+m) + 4n$ |
| Training speed | **Faster** | Slower |
| Long-term memory | Good | Slightly stronger on very long sequences |
| Gradient highway | Via $h_t = (1-z_t)h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ | Via $c_t = f_t c_{t-1} + i_t \tilde{c}_t$ |
| Use case preference | Smaller datasets, faster inference | Complex long-context tasks |
| Year proposed | **2014 (Cho et al.)** | 1997 (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GRU Cell Block Diagram (Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **internal data flow** of a single GRU cell at time step $t$, mapping every operation from raw inputs to the final hidden state output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["Input xt"]
    inH["Previous Hidden h t-1"]
    concat["Concatenation Layer"]
    matmulZ["Matrix Multiply Wz plus bz"]
    matmulR["Matrix Multiply Wr plus br"]
    matmulC["Matrix Multiply W plus b"]
    sigZ["Sigmoid Activation"]
    sigR["Sigmoid Activation"]
    tanhA["Tanh Activation"]
    hadamard1["Hadamard Product r t times h t-1"]
    hadamard2["Hadamard Product 1 minus z t times h t-1"]
    hadamard3["Hadamard Product z t times h tilde t"]
    sumOp["Element-wise Sum"]
    outH["Current Hidden h t"]
    outY["Optional Output y t"]

    inX --> concat
    inH --> concat
    concat --> matmulZ
    concat --> matmulR
    concat --> matmulC

    matmulZ --> sigZ
    matmulR --> sigR

    inH --> hadamard1
    sigR --> hadamard1
    hadamard1 --> matmulC
    inX --> matmulC

    matmulC --> tanhA

    inH --> hadamard2
    sigZ --> hadamard2
    hadamard2 --> sumOp

    sigZ --> hadamard3
    tanhA --> hadamard3
    hadamard3 --> sumOp

    sumOp --> outH
    outH --> outY
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **two inputs** ($x_t$ on top, $h_{t-1}$ on the side) merge into a **Concatenation Layer**.
- Three parallel branches compute $z_t$, $r_t$, and $\tilde{h}_t$ respectively.
- The **Reset Gate** modulates the past state via a Hadamard product *before* it enters the candidate state branch.
- The **Update Gate** performs a convex combination in the final sum.
- The output $h_t$ may feed into a task-specific output layer (e.g., a softmax classifier for next-word prediction).

## 4.2 Unrolled GRU Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1["GRU Cell at t-1"]
    t2["GRU Cell at t"]
    t3["GRU Cell at t+1"]
    xt1["x t-1"]
    xt2["x t"]
    xt3["x t+1"]
    ht1["h t-1"]
    ht2["h t"]
    ht3["h t+1"]

    xt1 --> t1
    t1 --> ht1
    ht1 --> t2
    xt2 --> t2
    t2 --> ht2
    ht2 --> t3
    xt3 --> t3
    t3 --> ht3
```

**Reading guide:** The horizontal arrows between cells represent the recurrent hidden state connection. Each cell receives its own time-step input and the previous hidden state, then emits the current hidden state — this is **unrolling through time**, the standard visualization for any RNN-based model.

## 4.3 Gate Behavior Schematic

| Gate | Value near 0 | Value near 1 | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Update Gate $z_t$** | Carry old state forward unchanged | Replace with new candidate | Acts as a *memory retention* switch |
| **Reset Gate $r_t$** | Erase past context before mixing | Use full past context | Acts as a *context relevance* filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Update Gate** in GRU plays the *combined* role of LSTM's **Forget Gate** and **Input Gate**. This is a frequent KTU exam question: *"Explain how the GRU update gate subsumes the functionality of the LSTM forget and input gates."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Define Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). List its gates and state the activation functions used by each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a variant of the standard RNN architecture proposed by Cho et al. (2014) to overcome the vanishing gradient problem in long sequences. It uses **two gates**:
>
> 1. **Update Gate ($z_t$)** — uses the **sigmoid ($\sigma$)** activation; controls how much of the previous hidden state should be retained.
> 2. **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** — uses the **sigmoid ($\sigma$)** activation; controls how much of the previous hidden state should be forgotten when computing the candidate state.
>
> The **candidate hidden state ($\tilde{h}_t$)** uses the **hyperbolic tangent ($\tanh$)** activation, and the **final hidden state ($h_t$)** is a linear interpolation with no non-linearity applied.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: Differentiate between GRU and LSTM based on number of gates, state variables, and parameter count.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | GRU | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | **2** (update, reset) | **3** (input, forget, output) |
| State variables | 1 (hidden state $h_t$) | 2 (cell state $c_t$ and hidden state $h_t$) |
| Parameters per cell | $3n(n+m) + 3n$ | $4n(n+m) + 4n$ |
| Training speed | Faster | Slower |
| Memory mechanism | Linear interpolation of states | Additive cell-state update |

GRU is a **simplified, more efficient alternative** to LSTM with **25% fewer parameters** for comparable performance on most sequence tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A 14-mark question on GRU typically allocates **4 marks for diagram**, **6 marks for equations with explanations**, and **4 marks for comparison/application**. Students who skip the **architectural diagram** lose easy marks. Always draw the cell with all **gates, weight matrices, and activation functions labelled** before writing equations.

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Draw and explain the architecture of a GRU cell. Write all the governing equations and explain the role of each gate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare GRU with LSTM in terms of architecture, parameters, gradient flow, and typical use cases. Discuss one real-world application where GRU is preferred over LSTM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (3 marks):**

A GRU cell at time step $t$ receives the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and the current input $x_t$. These are concatenated and passed through three parallel branches:

- **Update Gate branch** — produces $z_t$ via sigmoid.
- **Reset Gate branch** — produces $r_t$ via sigmoid and modulates $h_{t-1}$ via Hadamard product.
- **Candidate branch** — computes $\tilde{h}_t$ via $\tanh$.

The final output $h_t$ is a convex combination of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$ weighted by $z_t$.

**Governing Equations (3 marks):**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Role of Each Gate (1 mark):**

- **Update Gate $z_t$:** Acts as a *memory controller*, deciding the trade-off between retaining the past and adopting new input information.
- **Reset Gate $r_t$:** Acts as a *context eraser*, allowing the model to drop irrelevant past information before forming a new candidate state.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Architecture & Parameters (2 marks):**

GRU has **2 gates** and **1 state vector**; LSTM has **3 gates** and **2 state vectors** ($c_t$ and $h_t$). GRU has $3n(n+m) + 3n$ parameters; LSTM has $4n(n+m) + 4n$ parameters. Thus GRU is **25% more parameter-efficient**.

**Gradient Flow (2 marks):**

Both architectures create a *gradient highway*. In GRU, the term $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$ in the output equation allows gradients to flow back unchanged when $z_t$ is small. In LSTM, the cell-state update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ provides a similar but separate pathway through $c_t$.

**Use Case Preference (2 marks):**

GRU is preferred for **mobile/edge deployment**, **real-time speech recognition**, and **smaller datasets** where training speed is critical. LSTM remains the choice for very long-context tasks like document-level machine translation or complex music generation. A concrete example: **real-time keyword spotting on smart speakers** often uses GRU because of its lower latency.

**Application Discussion (1 mark):** Mention a real use case such as **GRU-based encoder-decoder for neural machine translation** (Cho et al. 2014 original paper), or **GRU in TensorFlow Lite for on-device speech recognition**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the four forward-pass equations of a GRU cell. Show how the update gate subsumes the functionality of the LSTM's forget and input gates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GRU cell has hidden size $n = 2$ and input size $m = 2$. Given weights and inputs as specified below, compute the output hidden state $h_t$. Show all intermediate computations. **(7 marks)**

> **Given:** $h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$, $x_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$,
>
> $$W_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix},\quad b_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$W_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix},\quad b_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix},\quad b = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Forward-Pass Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concatenation:**
$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] = [0.1,\ 0.2,\ 0.5,\ 0.3]^T$$

**Step 2 — Update Gate:**
$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

This projects the augmented vector through $W_z$ and squashes via sigmoid.

**Step 3 — Reset Gate:**
$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**Step 4 — Candidate State:**
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

**Step 5 — Final Hidden State:**
$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Update Gate Subsumes LSTM Forget + Input Gates (3 marks):**

In LSTM, the cell-state update is:
$$c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$$
where $f_t$ is the forget gate and $i_t$ is the input gate. Note the constraint $f_t + i_t = 1$ is *not* enforced.

In GRU, the hidden-state update is:
$$h_t = \underbrace{(1 - z_t)}_{\text{analog of } f_t} \odot h_{t-1} + \underbrace{z_t}_{\text{analog of } i_t} \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

Here, $(1 - z_t)$ directly acts as the **forget gate** (deciding what to retain from the past) and $z_t$ directly acts as the **input gate** (deciding what new information to add). Crucially, the GRU formulation *enforces* the complementary relationship $f_t + i_t = 1$ structurally — they are tied to a single $z_t$. This is the key architectural simplification of GRU over LSTM.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Concatenate inputs (0.5 mark):**
$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute Update Gate $z_t$ (2 marks):**
$$W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] = \begin{bmatrix} (0.1)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.3) \\ (0.2)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.05 + 0.06 \\ 0.02 + 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.03 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 \\ 0.17 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying sigmoid:
$$z_t = \sigma\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.16 \\ 0.17 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.16}} \\ \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.17}} \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.5399 \\ 0.5424 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute Reset Gate $r_t$ (1.5 marks):**
$$W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] = \begin{bmatrix} (0.1)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) \\ (0.2)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.3) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 + 0.02 + 0.05 + 0.03 \\ 0.02 + 0.04 + 0.10 + 0.06 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.11 \\ 0.22 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying sigmoid:
$$r_t = \sigma\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.11 \\ 0.22 \end{bmatrix}\right) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.5274 \\ 0.5548 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute Modulated Past State (0.5 mark):**
$$r_t \odot h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5274 \times 0.1 \\ 0.5548 \times 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0527 \\ 0.1110 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute Candidate State $\tilde{h}_t$ (1.5 marks):**
$$[r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0527 \\ 0.1110 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] = \begin{bmatrix} (0.3)(0.0527) + (0.1)(0.1110) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.3) \\ (0.2)(0.0527) + (0.3)(0.1110) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0158 + 0.0111 + 0.05 + 0.06 \\ 0.0105 + 0.0333 + 0.05 + 0.03 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1369 \\ 0.1238 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying tanh:
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.1369 \\ 0.1238 \end{bmatrix}\right) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.1361 \\ 0.1232 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Final Hidden State $h_t$ (1 mark):**
$$1 - z_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.5399 \\ 1 - 0.5424 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4601 \\ 0.4576 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4601 \times 0.1 \\ 0.4576 \times 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0460 \\ 0.0915 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5399 \times 0.1361 \\ 0.5424 \times 0.1232 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0735 \\ 0.0668 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0460 \\ 0.0915 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0735 \\ 0.0668 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1195 \\ 0.1583 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Answer:** $h_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.1195 \\ 0.1583 \end{bmatrix}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Problems:**
> 1. Always show the **concatenation step** explicitly (1 mark reserved).
> 2. For matrix multiplication, **expand each element** as a sum of products — do not skip intermediate arithmetic.
> 3. State the **activation function applied** at each gate (sigmoid vs tanh) — students often confuse which is used where.
> 4. The **Hadamard product** in Step 4 is commonly missed; writing $h_{t-1}$ directly into the candidate computation will cost 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for GRU (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4):**

- **Full form:** Gated Recurrent Unit — proposed by **Cho et al. (2014)**.
- **Number of gates:** **2** (Update Gate + Reset Gate).
- **State variables:** Only **one** hidden state vector $h_t$ (no separate cell state unlike LSTM).
- **Four canonical equations** (must memorize verbatim):
  - $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$
  - $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$
  - $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$
  - $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$
- **Activations:** Sigmoid for both gates, **tanh for candidate state**, **linear (identity) for final state**.
- **Parameter count:** $P_{\text{GRU}} = 3n(n+m) + 3n$ — **25% fewer** than LSTM.
- **Update Gate $z_t$ role:** Subsumes LSTM's *forget* and *input* gates with the constraint $f_t + i_t = 1$ enforced.
- **Reset Gate $r_t$ role:** Controls how much past context is forgotten before forming the candidate state.
- **Gradient highway:** Created by the term $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$ in the final equation — prevents vanishing gradients.
- **Hadamard product ($\odot$):** Element-wise multiplication — used in **two** places (reset modulation and final interpolation).
- **Concatenation $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$:** Always the first operation; required to produce a single augmented vector of dimension $(n+m)$.
- **Draw the diagram:** KTU allocates 3–4 marks for the cell architecture in 14-mark questions — always include it.
- **Applications:** Speech recognition, machine translation, time-series forecasting, music generation, IoT anomaly detection.
- **Key difference from LSTM:** GRU has **no output gate** and **no separate cell state** — the hidden state plays both roles.
- **Why choose GRU over LSTM:** Fewer parameters, faster training, better for edge deployment and smaller datasets.
- **Numerical problems:** Practice computing $z_t$, $r_t$, $\tilde{h}_t$, and $h_t$ step by step with given matrices and vectors.
- **Year of introduction:** **2014** (often confused with LSTM's 1997 — remember Cho et al. specifically).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of Application of Neural Networks in Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Application of Neural Networks in Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare Neural Networks** refer to deep learning architectures—primarily **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**, **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs/LSTMs)**, **Autoencoders**, **U-Nets**, and **Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)**—that are trained on biomedical data (medical images, Electronic Health Records, genomic sequences, physiological signals) to perform diagnosis, prognosis, segmentation, drug response prediction, and clinical decision support tasks.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4), this topic is presented as a **descriptive, application-oriented case-study module** where the theoretical architectures studied in Modules 1–3 are mapped onto **real-world biomedical problem statements**. Each case study is typically characterized by:

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Input modality** | Image (X-ray, MRI, CT, Histopathology), Signal (ECG, EEG), Tabular (EHR), or Sequence (DNA/Protein) |
| **Network family** | CNN for spatial data, RNN/LSTM for temporal data, U-Net for segmentation, GNN for molecular data |
| **Learning paradigm** | Supervised (most common), Self-supervised (SimCLR, MoCo), Transfer Learning (Inception, ResNet, DenseNet) |
| **Validation standard** | Comparison against board-certified clinicians / radiologists / dermatologists |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Neural Network as a Specialist Consultant"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine walking into a hospital where a junior doctor has read **millions of X-rays, MRIs, and patient records** overnight, remembers the *pattern* of every tumour, scar, and arrhythmia ever scanned, and yet still asks the senior consultant for a final opinion. That "junior doctor" is a deep neural network — pattern-recognition on a scale no human can match. The senior doctor (the clinician) remains in the loop, but the network pre-screens, prioritises, and quantifies risk.

* The **convolutional filters** act like a "magnifying glass with trained eyes" — they learn to look for *edges, textures, blobs* and finally *lesion shapes*.
* The **fully-connected head** acts like the "decision-making brain" — taking those features and saying *cancer* or *benign*.
* The **recurrent memory (LSTM)** acts like a "patient history reader" — it remembers yesterday's vitals to predict tomorrow's cardiac arrest.

## 1.3 Key Healthcare-Specific Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Core syllabus vocabulary that must be memorised verbatim for KTU exams:**
> * **Sensitivity (Recall / True Positive Rate):** $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — the model's ability to catch *sick* patients. **Critical in healthcare** because a missed cancer is catastrophic.
> * **Specificity (True Negative Rate):** $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ — ability to identify *healthy* patients correctly.
> * **AUC–ROC:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve — threshold-independent metric, **standard in clinical ML papers**.
> * **Dice Coefficient (F1 for segmentation):** measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth masks.
> * **Transfer Learning:** reusing a network pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuning on a small medical dataset — *the single most important practical technique in medical AI*.

## 1.4 Major Case Studies Mandated by KTU Module 4

The KTU syllabus groups healthcare applications into the following five canonical case studies:

1. **Case Study 1:** CNN-based **Skin Lesion (Melanoma) Classification** — Esteva et al. (Nature 2017).
2. **Case Study 2:** CNN-based **Pneumonia Detection from Chest X-Rays** — Rajpurkar et al. (CheXNet).
3. **Case Study 3:** U-Net based **Brain Tumour Segmentation from MRI** — Ronneberger et al. (MICCAI 2015).
4. **Case Study 4:** 1D-CNN / LSTM for **ECG Arrhythmia Detection** — PhysioNet / MIT-BIH dataset.
5. **Case Study 5:** LSTM / Attention for **In-ICU Mortality Prediction from EHR** — MIMIC-III dataset (Harutyunyan et al.).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A 14-mark KTU question on this topic almost always picks *one* of these five case studies and asks for (a) the network architecture + data pipeline, and (b) the loss function + performance metrics + clinical comparison.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case Study 1 — Melanoma Detection (Esteva et al., *Nature* 2017)

### Architecture
* **Backbone:** Inception-v3 (GoogLeNet family) pre-trained on **ImageNet (~1.28 M images, 1000 classes)**.
* **Transfer Learning:** the final 1000-class softmax is replaced by a **2-class softmax** (benign vs. malignant) and fine-tuned.
* **Input:** dermoscopic images resized to **$299 \times 299 \times 3$**.
* **Training data:** **129,450 clinical images** covering **2,032 diseases** (a 21× larger dataset than any prior public release).
* **Output classes:** keratinocyte carcinomas vs. benign seborrheic keratosis; malignant melanomas vs. benign nevi.

### Why CNN over hand-crafted features?
* Traditional CAD (Computer-Aided Diagnosis) relied on the **ABCD rule** — Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter. CNNs learn these implicitly and *discover additional patterns* (texture, vascularity, sub-dermal patterns) that humans cannot describe.

### Clinical Validation
* The CNN was benchmarked against **21 board-certified dermatologists** on two binary classification tasks.
* **Result:** CNN achieved **$72.1 \pm 0.9\,\%$ accuracy (task 1)** and **$55.5 \pm 1.7\,\%$ (task 2)** — matching the average dermatologist (71.3 % and 58.7 %).

## 2.2 Case Study 2 — CheXNet: Pneumonia Detection (Rajpurkar et al., 2017)

### Architecture
* **Backbone:** 121-layer **DenseNet** (Dense Convolutional Network) — every layer is connected to every other layer in a feed-forward fashion.
* **Output:** 14-class sigmoid (multi-label: each X-ray can carry *multiple* pathologies simultaneously — Atelectasis, Cardiomegaly, Effusion, Infiltration, Mass, Nodule, Pneumonia, etc.).
* **Dataset:** **NIH ChestX-ray14** — 112,120 frontal-view X-rays with 14 disease labels (weakly labelled via NLP-mining of radiology reports).

### Dense Connectivity — the Key Innovation
For a network of $L$ layers, DenseNet has $\dfrac{L(L+1)}{2}$ direct connections. The $l^{th}$ layer receives feature maps from all preceding layers:
$$x_l = H_l\bigl(\,[\,x_0,\, x_1,\, \dots,\, x_{l-1}\,]\,\bigr)$$
where $[\,x_0,\dots,x_{l-1}\,]$ denotes **concatenation** of feature maps.

### Performance
* F1 score of **0.435** (radiologists averaged 0.387) on the pneumonia task.
* CheXNet also computes a **class activation map (CAM)** to localise the lesion — making the system *explainable*.

## 2.3 Case Study 3 — U-Net for Brain Tumour Segmentation (Ronneberger et al., MICCAI 2015)

### Problem Type
* **Semantic segmentation** — every pixel must be classified as *tumour* or *background*.
* This is *not* classification; it requires **pixel-wise prediction**.

### The U-Net Architecture
The network has a symmetric **encoder–decoder (hourglass)** structure:

* **Encoder (Contracting Path):** successive blocks of $3{\times}3$ convolutions + ReLU + $2{\times}2$ max-pooling. Captures *context* (what is in the image?).
* **Decoder (Expanding Path):** successive blocks of $2{\times}2$ up-convolutions (transposed conv) + $3{\times}3$ convolutions + ReLU. Enables *precise localisation* (where exactly is the tumour?).
* **Skip Connections:** the feature map from encoder layer $i$ is *copied and concatenated* to decoder layer $L-i+1$. This preserves high-resolution spatial details that would otherwise be lost during downsampling.

### Loss Function — Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy + Dice
The Dice Coefficient between predicted mask $P$ and ground truth $G$ is:
$$\text{Dice}(P,G) = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \dfrac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i}{\sum_{i} p_i^2 + \sum_{i} g_i^2}$$
where $p_i, g_i \in \{0,1\}$ for pixel $i$. The Dice **loss** is $\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \text{Dice}$. In practice, the weighted sum $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}}$ is used to handle class imbalance (tumour pixels are far fewer than background).

## 2.4 Case Study 4 — ECG Arrhythmia Detection (PhysioNet / MIT-BIH)

### Input
* **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** — 48 half-hour excerpts of two-channel ambulatory ECG recordings from 47 subjects, sampled at **360 Hz**.
* 5 standard AAMI beat classes: **N (Normal), S (Supraventricular), V (Ventricular), F (Fusion), Q (Unknown)**.

### Architecture — 1D CNN
* Input: a fixed-length window of ECG samples, e.g. **$1 \times 360$** (1-D signal).
* Convolutional filters of length 5–11 operate along the *time* axis only.
* Stacked conv + max-pool layers extract morphological features (QRS complex, P-wave, T-wave).
* Fully-connected softmax outputs class probabilities.
* A bidirectional **LSTM** can be appended on top to model beat-to-beat rhythm dependencies.

### Why not a 2D CNN on the spectrogram?
* ECG is intrinsically 1-D; using a 1-D CNN drastically reduces parameters (~100 K vs. millions) and matches the signal's natural topology.

## 2.5 Case Study 5 — In-ICU Mortality Prediction (MIMIC-III, Harutyunyan et al., 2017)

### Input
* **MIMIC-III** — a publicly available critical-care database of **>58,000 ICU admissions** at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center.
* Multivariate time series: vital signs (HR, BP, SpO₂), lab results, medications, intake/output — 17 clinical variables sampled irregularly over 48 hours.

### Architecture
* **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** with input **imputation, normalisation, and time-step embedding**.
* The LSTM's hidden state at the final time step is fed to a sigmoid → probability of in-hospital mortality.
* A multi-task variant jointly predicts **mortality, length-of-stay, phenotyping, and decompensation** using 4 output heads sharing the LSTM backbone.

### Why LSTM?
* ICU data is a **time series with irregular sampling and missing values** — LSTMs handle both naturally via the gating mechanism (input, forget, output gates).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most-revised page for KTU Module 4 — memorise every row.**

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True Positive Rate | *Cancer screening* — must be high |
| $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True Negative Rate | *Confirming healthy* — must be high |
| $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | Diagnostic confirmation |
| $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P, R | Imbalanced classes (medical!) |
| $\text{Dice}(P,G) = \dfrac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i}{\sum_i p_i^2 + \sum_i g_i^2}$ | Segmentation overlap | Brain / lung tumour masks |
| $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert}$ | Intersection over Union | General segmentation |
| $x_l = H_l\bigl([x_0,\dots,x_{l-1}]\bigr)$ | Dense connectivity | DenseNet (CheXNet) |
| $f_{i,j,k} = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{m,n} W_{m,n,k} \cdot x_{i+m,\,j+n} + b_k\right)$ | Convolution + ReLU | All CNN backbones |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | Cross-entropy loss | Classification heads |
| $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}\, d(\text{FPR})$ | Area under ROC | Threshold-free clinical metric |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Clinical Decision Support (CDS):** Pre-screening tool to triage radiology worklists — *Stanford, Google DeepMind, and Aravind Eye Hospital* have deployed CNN-based screening at scale.
* **Telemedicine:** Smartphone-based skin lesion apps (SkinVision, DermAssist) and diabetic-retinopathy apps (IDx-DR — **first FDA-approved autonomous AI diagnostic**, 2018).
* **Drug Discovery:** AtomNet (Atomwise) uses 3D CNNs on voxelised molecular structures to predict binding affinity, reducing lead-discovery time from months to days.
* **Remote Patient Monitoring:** LSTM-based models on wearable ECG patches (Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile) detect atrial fibrillation in real time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Convolutional Operation on a Medical Image

The **2-D convolution** (with kernel $W$ of size $f \times f$, stride $s$, padding $p$) produces an output feature map $Y$ of size:
$$\text{output}_\text{size} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n + 2p - f}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$
where $n$ is the input image dimension. For the CheXNet pipeline, a $224 \times 224$ X-ray fed into a $7 \times 7$ kernel with stride $2$ and padding $3$ produces:
$$\left\lfloor \dfrac{224 + 2(3) - 7}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{223}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112$$
giving a $112 \times 112$ feature map. This is the standard **pre-activation size** at the entry of ResNet/DenseNet.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dice Loss from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC).** For binary masks $P, G$ over $N$ pixels:
$$\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a soft, differentiable form** (because predictions are probabilities, not hard 0/1):
$$\text{DSC}_{\text{soft}} = \dfrac{2 \sum_{i=1}^N p_i g_i}{\sum_{i=1}^N p_i^2 + \sum_{i=1}^N g_i^2}$$

*Numerator explanation:* $p_i g_i$ is $1$ only when both predicted and ground truth are "tumour" at pixel $i$. Summing gives the *soft* intersection.

*Denominator explanation:* $\sum p_i^2$ is the count of "predicted tumour" pixels (weighted by confidence); same for $g$.

**Step 3 — Define the loss** (to be minimised, so flip and subtract from 1):
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \text{DSC}_{\text{soft}}$$

**Step 4 — Why add cross-entropy?** Pure Dice loss is unstable when $\sum p_i^2 + \sum g_i^2$ is small (i.e., a mostly-background batch). Cross-entropy stabilises gradients:
