$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \bigl[g_i \log p_i + (1-g_i)\log(1-p_i)\bigr]}_{\text{BCE (pixel-wise)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\left(1 - \dfrac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i}{\sum_i p_i^2 + \sum_i g_i^2}\right)}_{\text{Dice loss}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Suppose a 100×100 slice has 200 tumour pixels ($g=1$) and the network predicts 180 of them correctly with confidence $0.9$ and 50 wrong ones with confidence $0.6$. Then:
$$\sum p_i g_i = 180 \times 0.9 = 162, \quad \sum p_i^2 = 180 \times 0.81 + 50 \times 0.36 = 145.8 + 18 = 163.8, \quad \sum g_i^2 = 200$$
$$\text{DSC}_{\text{soft}} = \dfrac{2 \times 162}{163.8 + 200} = \dfrac{324}{363.8} \approx 0.891$$
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - 0.891 = 0.109$$
A good model — Dice score above **0.8** is typically considered *clinically useful* for brain tumour segmentation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity, Specificity, F1 from a Confusion Matrix

Given a 2-class problem (e.g., melanoma vs. benign on 1000 test images) with confusion matrix:

|  | Predicted Malignant | Predicted Benign |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Malignant** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Benign** | FP = 30 | TN = 770 |

Compute step by step:

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} = \dfrac{180}{180+20} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

$$\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP} = \dfrac{770}{770+30} = \dfrac{770}{800} = 0.9625$$

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} = \dfrac{180}{180+30} = \dfrac{180}{210} \approx 0.857$$

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} = \dfrac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical interpretation:** A sensitivity of **0.90** means the model catches 9 out of 10 cancers — a strong screening tool. Specificity of **0.96** keeps the false-alarm rate low, avoiding unnecessary biopsies.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — LSTM Forget Gate and Its Use in ICU Time Series

The LSTM cell takes input $x_t$, previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$, and previous cell state $C_{t-1}$. The **forget gate** $f_t$ decides what information to discard:
$$f_t = \sigma\bigl(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\bigr)$$
where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid, $W_f$ the weight matrix. The **cell state update** is:
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$
with $\odot$ element-wise multiplication. In the ICU mortality task, the forget gate learns to *forget stale lab values* (e.g., a normal potassium from 3 days ago) and *remember trending values* (e.g., creeping lactate over 12 hours).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-likely table to appear in a 14-mark KTU question. Memorise the *columns*.**

| Case Study | Network | Input | Dataset Size | Loss | Headline Metric | Clinician Parity? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Melanoma** | Inception-v3 (CNN) | Dermoscopic image $299^2$ | 129,450 images | Categorical CE | Acc. $72.1\,\%$ | Yes |
| **Pneumonia (CheXNet)** | DenseNet-121 | Chest X-ray $224^2$ | 112,120 X-rays | Weighted BCE (14 labels) | F1 $= 0.435$ | Yes |
| **Brain Tumour** | U-Net | MRI slice $256^2$ | BraTS 2015 (~250 cases) | Dice + BCE | Dice $\approx 0.89$ | Surpasses |
| **ECG Arrhythmia** | 1D-CNN + BiLSTM | ECG window $1 \times 360$ | MIT-BIH (109,000 beats) | Categorical CE | Acc. $\approx 98\,\%$ | Yes |
| **ICU Mortality** | LSTM | 17 vars × 48 time-steps | MIMIC-III (58 K admissions) | BCE (sigmoid) | AUC $\approx 0.86$ | Comparable |

## 3.6 Why Transfer Learning is Non-Negotiable in Medical Imaging

**Derivation of dataset-size intuition:** Suppose a CNN with $M$ trainable parameters needs roughly **$10 \times M$** labelled samples to generalise. DenseNet-121 has $\sim 8 \times 10^6$ parameters, so naive training would need $\sim 80$ million labelled medical images — *impossible*. Transfer learning with $N_{\text{med}} \ll 10M$ works because:
* **Low-level filters** (edges, blobs, textures) are *universal* across natural and medical images.
* Only the **final fully-connected layers** need to be re-learned (typically $<10^5$ parameters), reducing the sample requirement by **$10\times$**.

This is the *theoretical foundation* of why Esteva et al. and Gulshan et al. (diabetic retinopathy) could succeed with only thousands — not millions — of medical images.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — High-Level Healthcare ANN Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Data Source] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Neural Network Backbone]
    C --> D[Prediction Head]
    D --> E[Post-processing]
    E --> F[Clinical Decision Support]

    A1[X-ray, MRI, CT, Histopathology] -.-> A
    A2[ECG, EEG, Vitals] -.-> A
    A3[EHR, Lab Reports] -.-> A
    A4[Genomic Sequences] -.-> A

    B1[Resize, Normalize, Augment] -.-> B
    C1[CNN for Images] -.-> C
    C2[RNN or LSTM for Sequences] -.-> C
    C3[U-Net for Segmentation] -.-> C
    C4[Transformer for EHR] -.-> C

    D1[Softmax Classification] -.-> D
    D2[Sigmoid Risk Score] -.-> D
    D3[Pixel-wise Mask] -.-> D

    E1[Threshold Tuning] -.-> E
    E2[Class Activation Map] -.-> E
    E3[Calibration] -.-> E

    F1[Report to Radiologist] -.-> F
    F2[Alert to ICU Doctor] -.-> F
    F3[Triage Priority] -.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** The central backbone (CNN/RNN/U-Net) is *task-agnostic*; only the input modality and prediction head change between case studies. This is the "one network, many tasks" philosophy of medical AI.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — U-Net Architecture (Brain Tumour Segmentation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input MRI Slice 256x256] --> E1[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    E1 --> P1[MaxPool 2x2]
    P1 --> E2[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    E2 --> P2[MaxPool 2x2]
    P2 --> E3[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    E3 --> P3[MaxPool 2x2]
    P3 --> BOT[Bridge: Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    BOT --> U1[UpConv 2x2]
    E3 --> SC1[Skip Connection 1] --> U1
    U1 --> D1[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    D1 --> U2[UpConv 2x2]
    E2 --> SC2[Skip Connection 2] --> U2
    U2 --> D2[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    D2 --> U3[UpConv 2x2]
    E1 --> SC3[Skip Connection 3] --> U3
    U3 --> D3[Conv 3x3 + ReLU x2]
    D3 --> OUT[1x1 Conv: Sigmoid Mask]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skip connections (SC1, SC2, SC3)** are the *defining feature* of U-Net. They feed high-resolution encoder features directly to the decoder, solving the information-loss problem caused by successive max-pooling. Removing them degrades Dice score by 5–15 %.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Transfer Learning Pipeline for Medical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ImageNet: 1.28M Images, 1000 Classes] --> A2[Pre-train CNN Backbone]
    A2 --> A3[Saved Weights]

    A3 --> B1[Remove Final FC Layer]
    B1 --> B2[Add New FC Layer: N medical classes]
    B2 --> B3[Option A: Freeze Backbone, Train FC only]
    B2 --> B4[Option B: Fine-tune All Layers with Low LR]

    B3 --> C1[Small Medical Dataset: 1K to 100K images]
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Train for 10 to 50 epochs]
    C2 --> C3[Validate on Held-out Test Set]
    C3 --> C4{Dice or AUC Acceptable?}
    C4 -- No --> B2
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[Deploy as Clinical Decision Support]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — LSTM for ICU Mortality Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[t=1: HR, BP, SpO2, Lactate] --> L1[LSTM Cell]
    T2[t=2: HR, BP, SpO2, Lactate] --> L1
    T3[t=3: HR, BP, SpO2, Lactate] --> L2[LSTM Cell]
    T2 --> L2
    T4[t=4: HR, BP, SpO2, Lactate] --> L2
    T5[t=T: ...] --> L3[LSTM Cell]
    L3 --> H[Final Hidden State hT]
    H --> S[Sigmoid Layer]
    S --> P[Probability of Mortality]
    P --> DEC{Threshold 0.5?}
    DEC -- Yes --> ALR[Alert ICU Team]
    DEC -- No --> CON[Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — CheXNet Multi-Label Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMG[Chest X-ray 224x224x3] --> CN1[Dense Block 1: 6 layers]
    CN1 --> TL1[Transition Layer: 1x1 Conv + 2x2 Pool]
    TL1 --> CN2[Dense Block 2: 12 layers]
    CN2 --> TL2[Transition Layer]
    TL2 --> CN3[Dense Block 3: 24 layers]
    CN3 --> TL3[Transition Layer]
    TL3 --> CN4[Dense Block 4: 16 layers]
    CN4 --> GAP[Global Average Pooling]
    GAP --> FC[Fully Connected: 14 units]
    FC --> SIG[Sigmoid Activations]
    SIG --> OUT[14 Independent Probabilities]
    OUT --> THR{Threshold 0.5}
    THR --> DX1[Atelectasis, Cardiomegaly, ... Pneumonia]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For exam diagrams:** Mermaid's `flowchart TD` (top-down) is the most reliable syntax. Always keep node IDs alphanumeric (e.g., `CN1`, `nodeA`) and labels *clean text only* (no bold, no italics) inside double quotes.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
*List and briefly explain any **three** major application areas of neural networks in healthcare. Mention the type of neural network used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Application 1 — Medical Image Classification (CNN):** Convolutional Neural Networks are used to classify dermoscopic images (melanoma vs. benign — *Esteva et al., Nature 2017*), chest X-rays (pneumonia — *CheXNet, Rajpurkar et al., 2017*), and histopathology slides. Pre-trained backbones (Inception-v3, DenseNet-121) with transfer learning are the standard pipeline.
>

LSTMs are used to predict in-ICU mortality from multivariate vital-sign time series in the MIMIC-III database.
>

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is **transfer learning**? Why is it particularly important when applying CNNs to medical imaging tasks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Transfer learning is the technique of taking a neural network **pre-trained on a large source dataset** (e.g., ImageNet with 1.28 M natural images) and **fine-tuning** it on a smaller target dataset (e.g., a few thousand medical images).
>
> **Importance — Two reasons (2 marks):**
> 1. **Data scarcity:** A DenseNet-121 has $\sim 8 \times 10^6$ parameters, theoretically requiring $\sim 80$ M labelled medical images — an unachievable number. Transfer learning reduces the effective parameter count needing re-training to $\sim 10^5$.
> 2. **Feature universality:** Low-level convolutional filters (edge, blob, texture detectors) are *domain-agnostic*. Pre-trained filters are excellent initialisers; only the final classification layer needs task-specific learning.
>
> *Reference architectures:* Esteva et al. (Inception-v3), CheXNet (DenseNet-121), Gulshan et al. (Inception-v3 for diabetic retinopathy).

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **QUESTION A (14 marks) — Case Study: CNN for Melanoma Detection** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **dataset, preprocessing, and CNN architecture** used by Esteva et al. (2017) for the binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant melanoma and benign nevi. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **training procedure, loss function, evaluation metrics**, and the **clinical comparison with board-certified dermatologists** that validates the system. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Dataset (2 marks):** The authors curated a dataset of **129,450 clinical images** spanning **2,032 different diseases**. The images came from **18 dermatology archives** across the US, Europe, and Asia. The two binary classification tasks were:
> * **Task 1:** Keratinocyte carcinoma vs. benign seborrheic keratosis.
> * **Task 2:** Malignant melanoma vs. benign nevi.
> Class imbalance was addressed by upsampling the minority class.
>
> **Preprocessing (1 mark):** Images were resized to **$299 \times 299 \times 3$** to match the Inception-v3 input. No aggressive augmentation (no rotation beyond $\pm 30^{\circ}$) was used because dermoscopic features are orientation-sensitive (hair, scale patterns).
>
> **Architecture (4 marks):** The base network was the **Inception-v3** architecture (GoogLeNet family) with **42 layers** and $\sim 25$ M parameters, pre-trained on ImageNet. The final 1000-class softmax was replaced with a **2-class softmax**. Training used:
> * **Optimiser:** Stochastic Gradient Descent with momentum 0.9.
> * **Learning rate schedule:** $10^{-4}$ initial, decayed by 0.5 every 4 epochs.
> * **Batch size:** 32.
> * **Epochs:** 30 (with early stopping).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Loss function (2 marks):** Standard **categorical cross-entropy**:
> $$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{2} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$
> with class weights to handle the malignant/benign imbalance.
>
> **Evaluation metrics (2 marks):** The authors used:
> * **Sensitivity (Recall)** — primary metric in clinical practice.
> * **Specificity** — to bound false alarms.
> * **Accuracy** — secondary, reported with **95 % confidence intervals** via bootstrap (1000 resamples).
>
> **Clinical Comparison (3 marks):** The model was tested on a held-out set of **300 images** and compared with **21 board-certified dermatologists** from Stanford. For Task 1, the CNN achieved **$72.1 \pm 0.9\,\%$ accuracy**, matching the dermatologists' average of **$71.3\,\%$**. For Task 2, the CNN reached **$55.5 \pm 1.7\,\%$** vs. dermatologists' **$58.7\,\%$** — within statistical parity. Crucially, the CNN maintained accuracy even on **out-of-distribution** images (different cameras, lighting), whereas dermatologist accuracy degraded.
>
> **Significance:** This was the first CNN to match dermatologist-level accuracy on a population-level skin-cancer task — published in *Nature*, 2017.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> * Students commonly lose **1–2 marks** by failing to mention the **input size ($299^2$)** or the **transfer-learning replacement of the final FC layer**.
> * Do **not** confuse this with Gulshan et al.'s diabetic-retinopathy paper (also Inception-v3, 2016) — they are different case studies.
> * A 1-mark penalty is applied if you write "GoogLeNet" without the version "Inception-v3".

---

### **QUESTION B (14 marks) — Case Study: U-Net for Brain Tumour Segmentation** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **U-Net architecture** for biomedical image segmentation. What is the role of **skip connections**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Formulate the **Dice loss function** and show, with a numerical example, how it is computed. Explain why **Dice loss is preferred over pure cross-entropy** in tumour segmentation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture (5 marks):** U-Net has a symmetric **encoder–decoder** (hourglass) structure. The encoder is a *contracting path* that captures context through successive $3{\times}3$ convolutions + ReLU + $2{\times}2$ max-pooling (downsampling by 2). At each pool, the number of feature channels doubles (e.g., $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$). The decoder is an *expanding path* that enables precise localisation through $2{\times}2$ **transposed convolutions** (upsampling by 2) + $3{\times}3$ convolutions, halving the channel count at each step. A **$1 \times 1$ convolution** at the output maps the final 64-channel feature map to the desired number of classes (2 for binary: tumour / background), followed by a softmax/sigmoid.
>
> **Skip Connections (2 marks):** At every stage of the decoder, the corresponding encoder feature map is *cropped to size* and **concatenated** to the decoder feature map. These skip connections allow the decoder to access high-resolution spatial information that was lost during downsampling. Without skip connections, fine-grained boundaries of the tumour (often only a few pixels wide) cannot be recovered, and the Dice score drops by 5–15 %.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Dice Coefficient Formulation (3 marks):** For predicted soft mask $P$ and ground-truth binary mask $G$ over $N$ pixels:
> $$\text{Dice}(P,G) = \dfrac{2 \sum_{i=1}^N p_i g_i}{\sum_{i=1}^N p_i^2 + \sum_{i=1}^N g_i^2}$$
> The Dice loss is $\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \text{Dice}$. Numerator: $2 \times$ soft intersection. Denominator: sum of squared foreground probabilities (predicted + ground truth) — a smooth proxy for the *cardinality* of the union.
>
> **Numerical Example (2 marks):** Suppose 10,000 pixels, 500 are tumour ($g_i=1$). The model correctly predicts 450 with $p=0.9$ and misses 50. It also wrongly predicts 100 background pixels as tumour with $p=0.6$. Then:
> $$\sum p_i g_i = 450 \times 0.9 = 405$$
> $$\sum p_i^2 = 450 \times 0.81 + 100 \times 0.36 = 364.5 + 36 = 400.5$$
> $$\sum g_i^2 = 500$$
> $$\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \times 405}{400.5 + 500} = \dfrac{810}{900.5} \approx 0.899, \quad \mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 0.101$$
>
> **Why Dice over pure Cross-Entropy? (2 marks):** Tumour pixels are typically **< 1 %** of a brain MRI slice — extreme class imbalance. Cross-entropy is dominated by the easy negatives (background), so the network learns to predict *all-background* and still achieves > 99 % pixel accuracy. Dice loss directly optimises the *overlap metric* and is invariant to the background/foreground ratio, producing sharp, complete masks.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> * Many students confuse **Dice Coefficient** with **IoU (Jaccard Index)**. Relation: $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Dice}}{2 - \text{Dice}}$. A Dice of 0.9 corresponds to IoU $\approx 0.82$.
> * Always state that Dice loss uses the *soft* (probability) form — not the hard 0/1 form — during training.
> * Writing "U-Net is a CNN" without mentioning **transposed convolution** in the decoder loses 1 mark.
> * A common mistake: confusing **U-Net** with **ResNet skip connections** — U-Net *concatenates* feature maps; ResNet *adds* them.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision list for the KTU Module 4 viva / written exam — read these bullets twice.**

* **Five canonical case studies** (memorise the *network*, *dataset*, and *headline metric* of each):
  1. Melanoma — **Inception-v3 + Transfer Learning** — 129,450 images — 72.1 % accuracy.
  2. Pneumonia — **DenseNet-121 (CheXNet)** — 112,120 X-rays — F1 $= 0.435$.
  3. Brain tumour — **U-Net** — BraTS — Dice $\approx 0.89$.
  4. ECG arrhythmia — **1D-CNN + BiLSTM** — MIT-BIH — 98 % accuracy.
  5. ICU mortality — **LSTM** — MIMIC-III — AUC $\approx 0.86$.

* **Transfer learning** is *the* enabler of medical AI — pre-train on ImageNet, fine-tune on medical data.

* **Metrics hierarchy in healthcare:** Sensitivity > Specificity > Accuracy (because missing a sick patient is worse than a false alarm).

* **U-Net's two innovations:** (i) symmetric encoder–decoder, (ii) **skip connections** (concatenation, not addition).

* **Dice loss** is preferred for imbalanced segmentation; cross-entropy is preferred for balanced classification.

* **CheXNet's innovation** is **dense connectivity** — $L(L+1)/2$ connections in $L$ layers, alleviating vanishing gradients without extra parameters.

* **LSTM's** three gates (forget, input, output) handle the irregular, missing, time-dependent nature of ICU data.

* **Validation against clinicians** — every KTU case study validates the network against board-certified experts (dermatologists, radiologists, ICU doctors) — always mention this in the conclusion.

* **FDA milestone:** IDx-DR (2018) was the **first fully autonomous AI diagnostic** approved by the FDA — for diabetic retinopathy screening.

* **Common KTU 1-mark trap:** confusing *sensitivity* with *specificity*; confusing *Dice* with *IoU*; confusing *Inception-v3* with *VGG-16* or *AlexNet*.
